- [x] For multi-digit ASCII strings, use the **Horner-style accumulation**: `result = result * 10 + new_digit`.
- [x] The first remainder obtained in repeated division is the **LSD (least significant digit)**, not the MSD.
- [x] BCD (Binary Coded Decimal) stores each decimal digit in 4 bits; a "45" stored in BCD is 0x45, NOT decimal 45 in binary.
- [x] Always validate the input range (0x30–0x39 for digits, 0x41–0x46 or 0x61–0x66 for hex letters) before conversion.
- [x] Stack-based reversal is preferred over array-index reversal when memory is scarce (8051 has only 128 bytes of internal RAM in the standard 8051).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Time Delay Generation and Relay Interface
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECL408 - MICROCONTROLLERS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Time Delay Generation and Relay Interface

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Time Delay Generation** in embedded systems refers to the precise production of a known, calculated waiting period using the **System Clock** and instruction-execution timing of the microcontroller. In the **MCS-51 (8051) family**, because every instruction consumes a deterministic number of **machine cycles**, a software loop can be used to "burn" a calculated amount of CPU time before the next operation is performed. The accuracy of this delay is governed by the **Oscillator Frequency ($f_{osc}$)** and the architecture's **12-clock per machine cycle** rule.

**Relay Interface** refers to the hardware-and-software subsystem that allows the low-current, low-voltage logic signals from a microcontroller I/O pin (e.g., 8051 Port 1, 3.3 V / 5 V, ~20 mA sink) to safely control a high-power electromagnetic load (e.g., 230 V AC lamp, motor, solenoid) by energising an **Electromagnetic Relay** through a current-amplifying **transistor driver** and a **freewheeling diode** for back-EMF protection.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Module 2, Experiment 2**
> Students *must* be able to:
> 1. Write an 8051 assembly/C subroutine that produces a delay in the **millisecond** range.
> 2. Calculate exact loop counts using $T_{delay} = \frac{12}{f_{osc}} \times N_{MC}$, where $N_{MC}$ is total machine cycles.
> 3. Design the relay driver circuit (transistor + base resistor + flyback diode).
> 4. Write and execute the complete C/ASM program to switch a relay ON/OFF at specific intervals.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **stopwatch built from sand trickling through an hourglass**:

| Concept | Stopwatch Analogy | Microcontroller Reality |
|---|---|---|
| Crystal oscillator ($f_{osc}=11.0592$ MHz) | Sand grain dropping rate (very fast, fixed) | Each oscillator tick = 1 clock period |
| Machine cycle (12 ticks) | One full pinch of sand falling | One basic "step" of the CPU |
| `DJNZ` loop instruction | Flipping the hourglass N times | Each flip burns 2 machine cycles |
| Total delay = time sand falls | Time trapped inside the loop | Time spent looping = desired delay |

**For the Relay**, think of a **light switch on a wall**. You (a human) cannot directly wire your hand to a 230 V line. Instead, the switch uses a *small mechanical finger* (the relay coil's magnetic pull) that is triggered by a tiny action — this is the same trick: a *tiny* microcontroller pin "tug" closes a *huge* high-voltage circuit.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why not just toggle a pin directly?**
> A microcontroller I/O pin can source/sink only ~10–20 mA. An electromagnetic relay coil typically needs **30 mA – 70 mA** at **5 V–12 V**. Connecting it directly will *burn out* the pin and the MCU. Hence, a **driver stage** is mandatory.

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Metrics

- **Standard 8051 oscillator:** $f_{osc} = 11.0592\ \text{MHz}$ (used because it yields exact 9600/19200 baud rates for UART). Alternative: **12 MHz**.
- **Machine Cycle frequency:** $f_{MC} = f_{osc} / 12$
- **For 11.0592 MHz:** $T_{MC} = 12 / 11.0592 \times 10^6 = 1.085\ \mu\text{s}$
- **For 12 MHz:** $T_{MC} = 12 / 12 \times 10^6 = 1.000\ \mu\text{s}$
- **Relay coil current (typical 5V DPDT):** ~30 mA – 70 mA
- **Freewheeling diode reverse voltage rating:** $\geq 2 \times V_{coil}$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 8051 Timing Architecture — Why "12 Clocks per Machine Cycle"?

The original **Intel 8051** divides the oscillator frequency by a fixed internal divider of **12**. Therefore:

$$f_{MC} = \frac{f_{osc}}{12}, \quad T_{MC} = \frac{12}{f_{osc}}$$

Every instruction consumes a fixed number of machine cycles (1, 2, or 4 MCs). For delay loops we almost always use the **`DJNZ Rn, label`** instruction because it:
- Is **2 bytes long** (opcode + operand)
- Takes **2 machine cycles** to execute
- Automatically decrements `Rn` and jumps if not zero — perfect counter logic.

## 2.2 Single-Loop Delay Logic

**Assembly skeleton:**
```asm
DELAY:  MOV  R7, #0FFH   ; 1 MC  — load counter (255)
LOOP:   DJNZ R7, LOOP    ; 2 MC  — decrement, jump if ≠0
        RET              ; 2 MC  — return to caller
```

**Logical steps (the "Why" and "How"):**
1. Load the inner register `R7` with maximum value `FFH` = 255.
2. Each `DJNZ` execution decrements `R7` by 1 and checks for zero.
3. The CPU stays inside the loop until `R7` becomes 0.
4. Therefore, `DJNZ` runs **255 times**.
5. After exiting, `RET` returns to the calling program.

## 2.3 Nested-Loop Delay Logic (for milliseconds)

A single 8-bit register can produce only ~553 µs max — too small. We **nest** two registers:

```asm
DELAY:   MOV   R6, #0FFH   ; outer counter (255)
BACK:    MOV   R7, #0FFH   ; inner counter (255)
HERE:    DJNZ  R7, HERE    ; inner loop  (2 MC × 255)
         DJNZ  R6, BACK    ; outer loop  (2 MC × 255)
         RET               ; 2 MC
```

This extends the delay into the **hundreds of milliseconds** range.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Memorise these three relations — they appear in **almost every KTU ESE question** on this topic.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Machine cycle period | $T_{MC} = \dfrac{12}{f_{osc}}$ | seconds | $f_{osc}$ in Hz |
| 2 | Time for a single `DJNZ` | $t_{DJNZ} = 2 \times T_{MC}$ | seconds | Always 2 MCs |
| 3 | Single-loop delay (n-iterations) | $T_{delay} = n \times 2 \times T_{MC}$ | seconds | Ignores setup/return overhead for simplicity |
| 4 | Nested-loop total MCs | $N_{MC} = (N_{outer} \times (N_{inner} \times 2 + 2)) + 1 + 2 + 2$ | MCs | Accounts for `MOV` outer, inner `MOV`, two `DJNZ`, `RET` |
| 5 | Required count for target delay | $n = \left\lfloor \dfrac{T_{target}}{2 \times T_{MC}} \right\rfloor$ | integer | Round down if not exact |
| 6 | Base resistor for driver BJT | $R_B = \dfrac{V_{CC} - V_{BE}}{I_B}$ | ohms | $V_{BE} \approx 0.7$ V |
| 7 | Base current required | $I_B = \dfrac{I_{coil}}{h_{FE(min)}}$ | A | Use **minimum** $h_{FE}$ from datasheet |
| 8 | Diode reverse rating | $V_R \geq 2 \times V_{coil}$ | V | Safety factor of 2 |

## 2.5 Engineering Utility — Where This Is Used in Industry

- **Blinking LEDs / indicator beacons** in control panels.
- **PWM-style soft start** for motors by varying delay between energisation steps.
- **Debounce routines** for mechanical switches (~10–20 ms).
- **Real-Time Clock (RTC)** tick generation when no hardware RTC exists.
- **Industrial relay banks** for HVAC, elevator control, traffic lights, home-automation, and PLC-style discrete outputs.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Single-Loop Maximum Delay @ 11.0592 MHz

We want the maximum delay produced by the single-loop routine:

```asm
DELAY:  MOV R7, #FFH     ; 1 MC
LOOP:   DJNZ R7, LOOP    ; 2 MC × 255
        RET              ; 2 MC
```

**Step 1: Compute machine cycle time.**

$$T_{MC} = \frac{12}{f_{osc}} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^6}$$

$$T_{MC} = 1.085\ \mu\text{s}$$

**Step 2: Sum total machine cycles.**

The inner `DJNZ` executes 255 times. The outer `MOV` runs once (1 MC). The `RET` runs once (2 MC). So total machine cycles:

$$N_{MC} = 1 + (255 \times 2) + 2$$

$$N_{MC} = 1 + 510 + 2 = 513\ \text{MCs}$$

**Step 3: Convert to time.**

$$T_{delay} = N_{MC} \times T_{MC} = 513 \times 1.085\ \mu\text{s}$$

$$T_{delay} = 556.605\ \mu\text{s} \approx 556.6\ \mu\text{s}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Nested-Loop Delay @ 11.0592 MHz

Using the nested routine with both R6 = R7 = FFH:

```asm
DELAY:   MOV  R6, #FFH     ; 1 MC  (outer load)
BACK:    MOV  R7, #FFH     ; 1 MC  (inner load, every outer iter)
HERE:    DJNZ R7, HERE     ; 2 MC × 255 = 510 MC
         DJNZ R6, BACK     ; 2 MC × 255 = 510 MC
         RET               ; 2 MC
```

**Step 1: Machine cycles for ONE outer iteration (R6 = 1 cycle).**

Inside one execution of "BACK": inner `MOV` (1 MC) + 255 × inner `DJNZ` (510 MC) + outer `DJNZ` (2 MC).

$$N_{per\ outer} = 1 + 510 + 2 = 513\ \text{MCs}$$

**Step 2: Total for all 255 outer iterations.**

$$N_{outer\ total} = 255 \times 513 = 130{,}815\ \text{MCs}$$

**Step 3: Add setup and return.**

Add initial `MOV R6` (1 MC) and final `RET` (2 MC):

$$N_{total} = 1 + 130{,}815 + 2 = 130{,}818\ \text{MCs}$$

**Step 4: Compute time.**

$$T_{delay} = 130{,}818 \times 1.085\ \mu\text{s}$$

$$T_{delay} = 141{,}937.53\ \mu\text{s} \approx 141.94\ \text{ms}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Loop Count for an Arbitrary Target Delay

**Problem:** Generate exactly **50 ms** delay using $f_{osc} = 11.0592$ MHz with a *single* loop using `R7`.

**Step 1:** $T_{MC} = 1.085\ \mu\text{s}$.

**Step 2:** Each `DJNZ` iteration consumes $2 \times T_{MC} = 2.17\ \mu\text{s}$.

**Step 3:** Required iterations.

$$n = \left\lfloor \frac{50{,}000\ \mu\text{s}}{2.17\ \mu\text{s}} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 23{,}041.47 \right\rfloor = 23{,}041$$

**Step 4:** Convert to hex.

$23{,}041_{10} = 5A01_{16}$ — does **not fit** in a single 8-bit register (max 255). Therefore, **a single loop is impossible** — we **must** use a nested loop.

**Step 5:** Outer `R6` × Inner `R7` estimation. Set `R6 = 200` and solve for `R7`.

$$\text{Inner MCs per outer iter} = 1 + (R7 \times 2)$$

$$200 \times (1 + 2 \cdot R7) + 3 = \frac{50{,}000}{1.085} = 46{,}082.9$$

$$200 + 400 \cdot R7 = 46{,}079.9$$

$$400 \cdot R7 = 45{,}879.9 \Rightarrow R7 \approx 114.7$$

Round down to **R7 = 114 = 0x72**. Final check confirms ~50.0 ms.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Relay Base Resistor

Given a 5 V relay coil drawing **70 mA**, driven by a **BC547** NPN with $V_{BE(sat)} = 0.7\ \text{V}$ and $h_{FE(min)} = 110$. The MCU pin delivers **5 V** at high.

**Step 1:** Required base current.

$$I_B = \frac{I_{coil}}{h_{FE(min)}} = \frac{70\ \text{mA}}{110} = 0.636\ \text{mA}$$

**Step 2:** Required base resistor.

$$R_B = \frac{V_{OH} - V_{BE}}{I_B} = \frac{5 - 0.7}{0.636 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$R_B = \frac{4.3}{6.36 \times 10^{-4}} = 6{,}760\ \Omega$$

**Step 3:** Choose standard E12 value: **$R_B = 6.8\ \text{k}\Omega$**.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Loop Type | Max Delay @ 11.0592 MHz | Max Delay @ 12 MHz | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| Single loop (R7 only) | 556.6 µs | 513 µs | LED blink, keypad scan |
| Double nested (R6, R7) | 141.94 ms | 130.82 ms | Relay toggle, debounce |
| Triple nested (R5, R6, R7) | ~36 s | ~33 s | Long interval timers |
| Timer0 mode 1 (16-bit) | 71.1 ms (single overflow) | 65.5 ms | Accurate sub-second timing |

> [!TIP]
> For *accurate* long delays in real applications, prefer **Hardware Timers (Timer0/Timer1 in Mode 1)** instead of pure software loops — software loops are blocked from interrupts and CPU cannot multitask.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Delay + Relay System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[POWER ON Reset] --> B[Initialise Port Pin P1.0 as OUTPUT]
    B --> C[Set P1.0 LOW - Relay OFF]
    C --> D[Call DELAY Subroutine]
    D --> E[DELAY Inner LOOP DJNZ R7]
    E --> F{DJNZ R6 Outer Loop}
    F -- Not Zero --> E
    F -- Zero --> G[Return from DELAY]
    G --> H[Set P1.0 HIGH - Energise Relay Coil]
    H --> I[Call DELAY Subroutine]
    I --> J[Set P1.0 LOW - De energise Relay]
    J --> K[Loop Forever]
    K --> D

    B1[8051 MCU Port Pin P1.0] --> C1[Base Resistor 6.8 k ohm]
    C1 --> D1[BC547 NPN Base]
    D1 --> E1[Collector connects to Relay Coil Terminal A]
    F1[Relay Coil Terminal B connects to VCC 5V]
    E1 --> G1[Freewheeling Diode 1N4007 across coil]
    H1[Relay COM terminal connects to Load Supply 230V AC]
    I1[Relay NO terminal connects to LOAD Lamp Motor]
```

## 4.2 Detailed Relay Driver Schematic (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    MCU[8051 Port Pin P1.0 OUTPUT 5V] --> RB[Base Resistor RB 6.8 kohm]
    RB --> BJT[BC547 NPN BJT]
    BJT -- Collector --> COIL[Relay Coil 5V 70 mA]
    COIL --> VCC[DC Supply 5V]
    BJT -- Emitter --> GND[Ground 0V]
    DIODE[1N4007 Flyback Diode] -. cathode to VCC .- COIL
    DIODE -. anode to collector .- BJT
    COIL -. magnetic flux .- SWITCH[Relay SPDT Contacts COM NO NC]
    SWITCH --> LOAD[230V AC LOAD Lamp]
```

## 4.3 Program Execution Flowchart

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Init
    Init --> RelayOFF: P1.0 = 0
    RelayOFF --> DelayCall: call delay
    DelayCall --> InnerLoop: R7 = FFH
    InnerLoop --> InnerCheck: R7--
    InnerCheck --> InnerLoop: R7 not zero
    InnerCheck --> OuterCheck: R7 zero
    OuterCheck --> InnerLoop: R6 not zero
    OuterCheck --> Return: R6 zero
    Return --> RelayON: P1.0 = 1
    RelayON --> DelayCall
    state DelayCall {
        [*] --> InnerLoop
    }
```

## 4.4 Annotated Hardware Pin Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph MICROCONTROLLER_8051
        PIN40[Vcc Pin 40 5V]
        PIN20[GND Pin 20]
        PIN18_XTAL1[XTAL1 Pin 18]
        PIN19_XTAL2[XTAL2 Pin 19]
        PIN1_PORT[P1.0 Pin 1 OUTPUT]
    end

    subgraph CRYSTAL_OSCILLATOR
        XTAL[Quartz Crystal 11.0592 MHz]
        C1[Capacitor 33 pF]
        C2[Capacitor 33 pF]
    end

    subgraph RELAY_DRIVER_STAGE
        RB[R 6.8 kohm]
        Q1[BC547 NPN]
        D1[1N4007 Diode]
        K1[Relay Coil 5V DPDT]
    end

    subgraph LOAD_SIDE
        L_AC[230V AC Mains]
        LAMP[Lamp 100W]
    end

    PIN18_XTAL1 --- XTAL
    PIN19_XTAL2 --- XTAL
    XTAL --- C1
    XTAL --- C2
    C1 --- PIN20
    C2 --- PIN20
    PIN1_PORT --> RB
    RB --> Q1
    Q1 --> K1
    D1 --- K1
    K1 --> PIN40
    K1 -. closes contact .- L_AC
    L_AC --> LAMP
```

> [!TIP]
> For **electrical isolation** between the MCU and 230 V AC side, replace the direct BJT drive with an **opto-coupler (e.g., PC817)** between the MCU pin and the BJT base. This protects the MCU from voltage transients on the load side — a common KTU viva question.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Explain the concept of machine cycle in 8051. How is it related to the oscillator frequency?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A *machine cycle* is the basic unit of time recognised by the 8051 CPU for executing any instruction. The 8051 divides its oscillator frequency $f_{osc}$ internally by 12 to derive the machine cycle frequency. Hence, the duration of one machine cycle is $T_{MC} = 12/f_{osc}$. For example, with $f_{osc} = 12$ MHz, one machine cycle = **1 µs**; with $f_{osc} = 11.0592$ MHz, $T_{MC} = 1.085\ \mu\text{s}$. Every 8051 instruction consumes 1, 2, or 4 machine cycles.

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** Why is a freewheeling diode connected across a relay coil in an 8051-based relay driver circuit?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
When the relay coil is de-energised, the collapsing magnetic flux induces a high reverse voltage spike (back-EMF, can exceed 200 V) that can damage the driving transistor. A **freewheeling diode** (typically **1N4007**) is connected in *reverse bias* across the coil (cathode to VCC, anode to collector) to provide a safe circulating path for the inductor current, thereby clamping the back-EMF and protecting the BJT/MCU pin.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A — 14 Marks**  *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]*

**Sub-part (a) [7 marks]:** Design an 8051 assembly language subroutine to generate a delay of **approximately 100 ms** using a crystal of **11.0592 MHz**. Show all calculations.

**Sub-part (b) [7 marks]:** Draw the relay interfacing circuit with the 8051, including the driver transistor and freewheeling diode. Write the complete 8051 C program to toggle a relay connected to **P1.7** every 100 ms.

---

### **Solution to Question A**

#### (a) Sub-part Solution [7 marks]

**Step 1: Compute machine cycle time.**  
$T_{MC} = 12 / 11.0592 \times 10^6 = 1.085\ \mu\text{s}$

**Step 2: Target delay in machine cycles.**  
$N_{MC} = 100{,}000\ \mu\text{s} / 1.085\ \mu\text{s} = 92{,}165.9 \approx 92{,}166\ \text{MCs}$

**Step 3: Use a double-nested loop with R6 (outer) and R7 (inner).**

Total MCs formula:
$$N_{total} = 1 + N_6 \times (1 + 2 \cdot N_7) + 2 \cdot N_6 + 2$$

Ignoring initial `MOV R6` and final `RET` overhead for design:
$$N_6 \times (3 + 2 \cdot N_7) \approx 92{,}166$$

Choose **N₆ = 255, R6 = FFH**, solve for **N₇**:
$$255 \times (3 + 2 \cdot N_7) = 92{,}166$$
$$765 + 510 \cdot N_7 = 92{,}166$$
$$N_7 = \frac{92{,}166 - 765}{510} = 179.3$$

Choose **N₇ = 179 = 0xB3**.

**Final Subroutine:**
```asm
DELAY:  MOV   R6, #0FFH    ; 1 MC — outer = 255
BACK:   MOV   R7, #0B3H    ; 1 MC — inner = 179
HERE:   DJNZ  R7, HERE     ; 2 MC × 179 = 358 MC
        DJNZ  R6, BACK     ; 2 MC × 255
        RET                ; 2 MC
```

**Verification:**
$$N_{total} = 1 + 255 \times (1 + 358) + 510 + 2 = 1 + 91{,}597 + 510 + 2 = 92{,}110\ \text{MCs}$$

$$T_{delay} = 92{,}110 \times 1.085\ \mu\text{s} = 99{,}939.35\ \mu\text{s} \approx 99.94\ \text{ms} \approx 100\ \text{ms}$$

*(Acceptable tolerance ±5% for KTU marking)*

#### (b) Sub-part Solution [7 marks]

**Hardware Circuit (refer Section 4.2):** Port pin **P1.7** → **6.8 kΩ resistor** → **BC547 base**; collector → relay coil → +5V; emitter → GND; **1N4007** across coil (cathode to +5V).

**Complete 8051 C Program (Keil µVision syntax):**
```c
#include <reg51.h>

sbit RELAY = P1^7;        // Relay connected to P1.7

void delay_100ms(void)    // 100 ms delay subroutine
{
    unsigned char i, j;
    for(i = 0; i < 255; i++)
        for(j = 0; j < 179; j++);
}

void main(void)
{
    RELAY = 0;            // Initially OFF
    while(1)
    {
        RELAY = 1;        // Energise relay
        delay_100ms();    // Wait 100 ms
        RELAY = 0;        // De-energise relay
        delay_100ms();    // Wait 100 ms
    }
}
```

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (ALTERNATIVE CHOICE)**  *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]*

**Sub-part (a) [7 marks]:** Calculate the maximum delay that can be generated by a double-nested loop using registers R5 (outer) and R6 (inner) for an 8051 system with $f_{osc} = 12\ \text{MHz}$. Assume each `DJNZ` consumes 2 machine cycles.

**Sub-part (b) [7 marks]:** Interface a **12 V, 100 mA** relay to 8051 port P1.0. Design the driver circuit using a **BC547** ($h_{FE(min)} = 100$) with proper base resistor and freewheeling diode. Compute the resistor value.

---

### **Solution to Question B**

#### (a) Sub-part Solution [7 marks]

**Given:** $f_{osc} = 12\ \text{MHz}$, hence $T_{MC} = 12/12 \times 10^6 = 1\ \mu\text{s}$.

**Loop structure:**
```asm
DELAY:  MOV   R5, #0FFH   ; 1 MC outer
BACK:   MOV   R6, #0FFH   ; 1 MC inner per outer iter
HERE:   DJNZ  R6, HERE    ; 2 MC × 255
        DJNZ  R5, BACK    ; 2 MC × 255
        RET               ; 2 MC
```

**Total machine cycles:**
$$N_{MC} = 1 + 255 \times (1 + 255 \times 2) + 255 \times 2 + 2$$

Computing inner part: $1 + 510 = 511$ MCs per outer iteration.
$$N_{MC} = 1 + 255 \times 511 + 510 + 2$$
$$N_{MC} = 1 + 130{,}305 + 510 + 2 = 130{,}818\ \text{MCs}$$

**Maximum delay:**
$$T_{delay} = 130{,}818 \times 1\ \mu\text{s} = 130.818\ \text{ms}$$

#### (b) Sub-part Solution [7 marks]

**Step 1: Compute base current required.**
$$I_B = \frac{I_{coil}}{h_{FE(min)}} = \frac{100\ \text{mA}}{100} = 1.0\ \text{mA}$$

**Step 2: Base resistor for 5 V logic high from MCU.**
$$R_B = \frac{V_{OH} - V_{BE}}{I_B} = \frac{5 - 0.7}{1.0 \times 10^{-3}} = 4.3\ \text{k}\Omega$$

**Step 3: Standard E12 value → $R_B = 4.3\ \text{k}\Omega$.**

**Step 4: Freewheeling diode rating.**
$$V_R \geq 2 \times V_{coil} = 2 \times 12 = 24\ \text{V}$$
Choose **1N4007** ($V_R = 1000\ \text{V}$, well above requirement).

**Step 5: Coil supply.** Since relay needs 12 V, use a separate **12 V supply** for the coil, NOT the 5 V MCU rail. Connect +12 V → relay coil → collector of BC547 → emitter → GND. The diode cathode goes to +12 V, anode to collector.

**Connection summary:**
- P1.0 → 4.3 kΩ → BC547 base
- BC547 collector → Relay coil terminal 1
- Relay coil terminal 2 → +12 V
- BC547 emitter → GND
- 1N4007: cathode → +12 V, anode → collector

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Mistakes in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Forgetting the divide-by-12 rule** — students write $T_{MC} = 1/f_{osc}$ instead of $12/f_{osc}$. Result: delay answers are off by 12×. **Penalty: 2–3 marks.**
> 2. **Not counting the outer `MOV` and `RET`** in total machine cycles. **Penalty: 1 mark.**
> 3. **Diode direction reversed in circuit diagram** — must be reverse-biased under normal operation (cathode toward +V). **Penalty: 1 mark.**
> 4. **Using a single 8-bit register for delays >1 ms** without nested loop. **Penalty: 2 marks** for not recognising register overflow.
> 5. **Confusing NO and NC relay contacts** in viva. NO = Normally Open (open when coil OFF). NC = Normally Closed (closed when coil OFF). **Penalty: 1 mark.**
> 6. **Forgetting to declare pin as output** in C program (`RELAY = 0` without `sbit` direction setup — 8051 ports default to FFh/input on reset). **Penalty: 1 mark.**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Machine Cycle Definition:** Always $T_{MC} = 12 / f_{osc}$. Master this relation first.
- **Most common crystal:** $11.0592\ \text{MHz}$ → $T_{MC} = 1.085\ \mu\text{s}$.
- **`DJNZ Rn, label`** is the *workhorse* delay instruction — 2 bytes, 2 machine cycles.
- **Maximum single-loop delay** @ 11.0592 MHz ≈ **556.6 µs** (with R7 only).
- **Maximum double-loop delay** @ 11.0592 MHz ≈ **141.94 ms** (with R6, R7).
- **Triple-nested loop** can stretch to seconds but blocks all interrupts.
- **For longer precise delays** → use **Hardware Timer in Mode 1 (16-bit)**.
- **Relay coil** must NOT be driven directly from MCU pin — always use a **BJT driver** (BC547/2N2222) or **ULN2003** driver IC array.
- **Base resistor formula:** $R_B = (V_{OH} - V_{BE}) \times h_{FE(min)} / I_{coil}$.
- **Freewheeling diode** is *mandatory* — reverse-biased, cathode to +V, anode to collector.
- **For 230 V AC loads**, isolate MCU from line side using **opto-coupler** (PC817/MOC3021).
- **Pin declaration in C:** Use `sbit RELAY = P1^7;` and the port direction is handled automatically by `sfr P1` on standard 8051.
- **Default port state after RESET:** All 8051 ports are **FFh (input mode, high-impedance)** — must write 0 to make pin low, 1 to make pin high after configuring as output.
- **Application link:** A typical *automatic room-light controller* uses this exact experiment — PIR sensor input + relay output + delay-off timer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Display (LED/Seven Segments/LCD) and Keyboard Interface
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECL408 - MICROCONTROLLERS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Display (LED/Seven Segments/LCD) and Keyboard Interface

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Display and Keyboard Interface** in the context of embedded systems and microcontroller-based design refers to the hardware and software methodologies employed to establish reliable, real-time bidirectional communication between a microcontroller unit (MCU) — such as the **Intel 8051** family or the **ARM Cortex-M** based boards (LPC2148 / STM32) — and human-interface peripherals. This includes:

1. **Visual Output Devices:** Light Emitting Diodes (LEDs), Seven-Segment Displays (SSD), and Liquid Crystal Displays (LCDs — primarily alphanumeric 16×2 HD44780-based modules).
2. **Input Devices:** Matrix Keyboards (typically 4×4 or 4×3 configurations) constructed using push-button switches arranged in a row-column grid.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned), the lab experiment evaluates the student's ability to **configure General Purpose Input/Output (GPIO) ports**, **implement polling/ISR-based scanning algorithms**, **manage timing constraints**, and **handle hardware-software co-design issues** such as switch bouncing, current limiting, and multiplexing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This experiment is mandatory under Module Experiment 2 of PCECL408. Students are expected to write, compile (using Keil µVision / LPCXpresso / STM32CubeIDE), simulate (using Proteus / EdSim51), and demonstrate working firmware for at least two of the following three peripheral combinations: (i) LED matrix, (ii) Seven-Segment multiplexing, (iii) LCD + Matrix Keypad.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of the microcontroller as a **post office clerk** sitting inside a sealed room. The clerk cannot see the outside world directly, but they have a set of **letter slots (GPIO pins)** on the wall.

- **LEDs** are like tiny **lamps** on the wall that the clerk can turn ON or OFF by sending a "1" or "0" into specific slots. A single bit controls one lamp.
- **A Seven-Segment Display** is like a **digital alarm clock** made of 7 small lamp bars arranged in the shape of the digit "8". By selectively turning ON specific bars (segments a–g) and turning OFF others, the clerk can "draw" any decimal digit (0–9) or a few alphabets (A, b, C, d, E, F).
- **An LCD** is like a **television screen** with a fixed grid (16 columns × 2 rows of characters). The clerk sends **ASCII codes** for letters/digits, plus a few **command codes** (like "go to column 5", "clear screen"), and the LCD's internal controller chip (HD44780) handles the actual rendering.
- **A Matrix Keypad** is like a **piano keyboard** with keys arranged in rows and columns. The clerk repeatedly asks each column: *"Are you being pressed?"* If a row responds "Yes" while a column is being asked, the clerk knows exactly which key was pressed. This is called **matrix scanning**.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Metric | Standard Value | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Forward Voltage (V_f) of a standard Red LED** | **1.8 V to 2.2 V** | Volts (DC) |
| **Standard LED Forward Current (I_f)** | **10 mA to 20 mA** | Milliamperes |
| **Series Resistor for 5V MCU → LED** | **220 Ω to 330 Ω** | Ohms (R = (V_supply − V_f) / I_f) |
| **LCD Operating Voltage (V_dd)** | **+4.5 V to +5.5 V** | Volts (TTL compatible) |
| **LCD Contrast Adjustment (V_EE)** | **0 V to +5 V** (via potentiometer) | Volts (negative for TN displays) |
| **Matrix Keypad Typical Resistance (contact)** | **< 50 Ω** (pressed), **> 10 MΩ** (open) | Ohms |
| **Switch Bounce Duration (mechanical)** | **5 ms to 20 ms** | Milliseconds |
| **Standard Refresh Rate for Multiplexed SSD** | **> 60 Hz** (per common line) | Hertz |
| **HD44780 Instruction Execution Time** | **37 µs** (most commands), **1.53 ms** (clear/home) | Microseconds |
| **8051 Machine Cycle @ 11.0592 MHz Crystal** | **1.085 µs** | Microseconds |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 11.0592 MHz crystal?** This frequency yields an exact 9600 baud rate for UART communication and produces a convenient 1.085 µs machine cycle. Using 12 MHz gives 1 µs machine cycle but introduces UART baud rate error.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown — LED Interfacing

**Conceptual Logic Flow:**

1. **Determine Logic Polarity:** A microcontroller pin sourcing **HIGH (5V/3.3V)** can directly drive an LED whose **cathode is grounded** through a current-limiting resistor. Conversely, a pin can **sink current** by outputting LOW with the LED's anode tied to Vcc.
2. **Current Limiting Resistor Calculation:** Without a resistor, the LED draws excessive current, damaging both the LED and the MCU pin. The resistor value is computed using **Ohm's Law**.
3. **Port Configuration:** Configure the GPIO pin as **output**. In 8051, writing `1` to a P1 pin makes it HIGH (quasi-bidirectional). For high-current loads, a driver IC like **ULN2003** or transistor (BC547) is mandatory.
4. **Software Pattern:** A simple `P1 = 0xFF;` (all HIGH) or `P1 = 0x00;` (all LOW) toggles an entire 8-LED bank.

**The "Why" behind each step:**

- LEDs are **non-ohmic devices** — their resistance changes nonlinearly with voltage. Without current limiting, they enter **thermal runaway**.
- MCU GPIO pins typically have a maximum sourcing/sinking current of **10–25 mA per pin** and **~80–100 mA per port**. Exceeding these damages the silicon.

### 2.2 Seven-Segment Display — Multiplexing Theory

A seven-segment display has **7 segments** (a, b, c, d, e, f, g) plus an optional **decimal point (dp)**, all arranged to form an "8" pattern.

**Two Hardware Variants:**

| Type | Common Pin Connection | Segment ON Condition | Logic Polarity |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Common Cathode (CC)** | All cathodes tied to **GND** | Apply **HIGH** to segment anode | Active HIGH |
| **Common Anode (CA)** | All anodes tied to **+Vcc** | Apply **LOW** to segment cathode | Active LOW |

**Multiplexing (Why and How):**

To display a multi-digit number (e.g., a 4-digit counter from 0000 to 9999), using one port per digit (4 ports × 8 bits = 32 pins) is wasteful. Instead, we use **time-multiplexing**:

1. Connect the **8 segment lines (a–g + dp)** of all digits in **parallel** to a single MCU port (e.g., P0).
2. Connect the **common pin of each digit** to a separate MCU pin (transistor-driven for higher current).
3. Rapidly activate **one digit at a time** (e.g., digit 1 ON, others OFF), show its segment pattern, then move to digit 2, and so on.
4. The human eye's **persistence of vision** (~24 Hz refresh threshold) perceives all digits as simultaneously lit.

**Refresh Rate Formula:**

$$
f_{refresh} = \frac{1}{T_{per\_digit} \times N_{digits}}
$$

For **N = 4 digits** and a desired refresh rate of **f ≥ 100 Hz** (to avoid flicker), the time per digit is:

$$
T_{per\_digit} \leq \frac{1}{100 \times 4} = 2.5 \text{ ms}
$$

### 2.3 LCD (16×2 HD44780) Interfacing

The **HD44780** is an industry-standard LCD controller. It supports two interface modes:
- **4-bit mode** (uses 4 data lines + 3 control lines = **7 pins total**) — most common in KTU labs.
- **8-bit mode** (uses 8 data lines + 3 control lines = **11 pins total**) — simpler but pin-expensive.

**Three Control Lines:**

| Signal | Full Form | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **RS** | Register Select | RS=0 → Command Register; RS=1 → Data Register |
| **R/W** | Read/Write | R/W=0 → Write to LCD; R/W=1 → Read from LCD (busy flag) |
| **EN** | Enable | A HIGH-to-LOW pulse latches data into LCD |

**Important LCD Commands (KTU Frequently Asked):**

| Hex Code | Command | Execution Time |
|---|---|---|
| **0x01** | Clear Display | **1.53 ms** |
| **0x02** | Return Home | **1.53 ms** |
| **0x0C** | Display ON, Cursor OFF | 37 µs |
| **0x0E** | Display ON, Cursor ON | 37 µs |
| **0x0F** | Display ON, Cursor Blinking | 37 µs |
| **0x28** | 4-bit mode, 2 lines, 5×7 font | 37 µs |
| **0x38** | 8-bit mode, 2 lines, 5×7 font | 37 µs |
| **0x80** | Set DDRAM address to (0,0) | 37 µs |
| **0xC0** | Set DDRAM address to (0,1) second line | 37 µs |
| **0x06** | Increment cursor (entry mode) | 37 µs |

**Initialization Sequence (4-bit mode) — KTU Critical:**

1. Wait **≥ 15 ms** after power-on.
2. Send `0x03` (function set — 8-bit) three times with delays.
3. Send `0x02` to switch to **4-bit mode**.
4. Send `0x28` (4-bit, 2-line, 5×7).
5. Send `0x0C` (display ON, cursor OFF).
6. Send `0x01` (clear display).
7. Send `0x06` (entry mode — auto increment).

### 2.4 Matrix Keypad — Scanning Algorithm

A **4×4 matrix keypad** uses **4 rows + 4 columns = 8 MCU pins** to interface 16 keys.

**Scanning Algorithm (Row scanning method):**

1. Configure all **row pins as OUTPUT** (drive them LOW one at a time).
2. Configure all **column pins as INPUT** (with internal pull-ups enabled).
3. Drive **Row 1 LOW**, others HIGH. Read all column pins.
4. If any column reads LOW, the key at the intersection of Row 1 and that column is pressed.
5. Repeat for Row 2, Row 3, Row 4.
6. Debounce the press using a **software delay (~20 ms)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Alternate Method (Column Scanning):** Drive all columns LOW one at a time, read rows. Both methods are functionally equivalent; choose whichever your KTU textbook/Viva question prefers.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Current Limiting Resistor for LED** | $R = \dfrac{V_{supply} - V_f}{I_f}$ | LED interfacing to MCU pin |
| **Power Dissipated in Resistor** | $P_R = I_f^2 \times R$ | Resistor wattage selection (typically 1/4 W) |
| **LED Forward Current (typical)** | $I_f = 10 \text{ mA}$ | Default assumption for standard 5mm LED |
| **Refresh Rate for Multiplexed SSD** | $f_{refresh} = \dfrac{1}{T_{per\_digit} \times N_{digits}}$ | Avoid display flicker |
| **Debounce Delay** | $T_{debounce} \geq 20 \text{ ms}$ | Software debounce for mechanical keys |
| **LCD Busy Flag Check** | Read BF (DB7) while R/W=1, RS=0 | Wait-state insertion for slow operations |
| **HD44780 Clear Command Wait** | $T_{clear} \geq 1.53 \text{ ms}$ | Mandatory delay after `0x01` command |
| **8051 Timer Overflow (Mode 1)** | $T_{overflow} = (65536 - X) \times T_{machine}$ | Delay generation using timers |
| **Debounced Key Counter** | $counter = (counter \times 10) + key$ | Building multi-digit number from keypad |
| **BCD-to-Seven Segment LUT** | Lookup table indexed by digit | Avoids runtime computation |

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Series Resistor Value for LED Interfacing

**Problem Statement:** A red LED (V_f = 2.0 V, I_f = 15 mA) is connected to a 8051 P1.0 pin (V_OH = 5.0 V) through a series resistor. Calculate the required resistor value and the power it dissipates.

**Step 1: Identify the circuit topology and apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL).**

The circuit forms a series loop: MCU pin → Resistor → LED anode → LED cathode → GND.

$$
V_{supply} = V_R + V_f
$$

**Step 2: Solve for the voltage drop across the resistor.**

$$
V_R = V_{supply} - V_f
$$

$$
V_R = 5.0 \text{ V} - 2.0 \text{ V} = 3.0 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 3: Apply Ohm's Law to compute the resistance.**

$$
R = \frac{V_R}{I_f}
$$

$$
R = \frac{3.0 \text{ V}}{15 \text{ mA}} = \frac{3.0}{0.015} = 200 \text{ Ω}
$$

**Step 4: Select the nearest standard E12 resistor value.**

E12 standard values: 10, 12, 15, 18, 22, 27, 33, 39, 47, 56, 68, 82, 100, 120, 150, 180, 220, 270, 330...

The closest standard value is **220 Ω**. This results in a slightly lower current:

$$
I_{f,actual} = \frac{V_R}{R_{actual}} = \frac{3.0 \text{ V}}{220 \text{ Ω}} \approx 13.6 \text{ mA}
$$

This is well within the safe operating range of the LED.

**Step 5: Compute power dissipation in the resistor.**

$$
P_R = I_{f,actual}^2 \times R_{actual}
$$

$$
P_R = (0.0136)^2 \times 220 = 0.000185 \times 220 \approx 0.041 \text{ W}
$$

Since this is well below 1/4 W (= 0.25 W), a standard **1/4 W carbon film resistor** is adequate.

### 3.2 Derivation: Optimal Multiplexing Delay for 4-Digit SSD

**Problem Statement:** Design a multiplexed 4-digit seven-segment display using 8051 @ 11.0592 MHz. Compute the delay to be inserted between digit switches to ensure a flicker-free display.

**Step 1: Choose a target refresh rate.**

For a stable visual perception, we select:

$$
f_{refresh} = 200 \text{ Hz}
$$

This is well above the 60 Hz flicker fusion threshold.

**Step 2: Compute the time available per digit (full cycle divided by 4).**

$$
T_{cycle} = \frac{1}{f_{refresh}} = \frac{1}{200} = 5 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
T_{per\_digit} = \frac{T_{cycle}}{N_{digits}} = \frac{5 \text{ ms}}{4} = 1.25 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 3: Convert this delay into 8051 Timer 0 counts (Mode 1, 16-bit).**

Machine cycle time at 11.0592 MHz:

$$
T_{machine} = \frac{12}{f_{oscillator}} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^6} \approx 1.085 \text{ µs}
$$

Number of machine cycles needed for 1.25 ms:

$$
N_{cycles} = \frac{T_{per\_digit}}{T_{machine}} = \frac{1250 \text{ µs}}{1.085 \text{ µs}} \approx 1152 \text{ cycles}
$$

**Step 4: Compute the timer reload value.**

For 16-bit Timer 0 in Mode 1, the timer counts up from the loaded value to 65536 (overflow):

$$
Timer\_Reload = 65536 - N_{cycles} = 65536 - 1152 = 64384
$$

Convert 64384 to hex: 64384 = 0xFBA0

So the high byte (TH0) = 0xFB, low byte (TL0) = 0xA0.

**Step 5: Derive the segment code for the digit "5" (Common Cathode).**

Digit "5" lights segments **a, c, d, f, g**.

| Segment | a | b | c | d | e | f | g |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| ON? | 1 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 1 |

Bit order (MSB → LSB = dp, g, f, e, d, c, b, a):

$$
Code = 0b01101101 = 0x6D
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Display Technologies

| Parameter | LED (Single) | Seven-Segment | 16×2 LCD (HD44780) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Information Density** | 1 bit | 1 decimal digit | 32 characters |
| **MCU Pins Required (typical)** | 1 per LED | 8 (multiplexed) | 6–7 (4-bit) |
| **Refresh Required** | No | Yes (multiplexing) | No (built-in controller) |
| **Power Consumption** | Low (~10 mA each) | Moderate | Very Low (~1–2 mA) |
| **Visibility in Sunlight** | Excellent | Excellent | Poor (needs backlight) |
| **Display of Alphabets** | No | Limited (A, b, C, d, E, F) | Full ASCII |
| **Cost per Unit** | Very Low | Low | Moderate |
| **KTU Viva Frequency** | High | High | Very High |

### 3.4 Step-by-Step: LCD 4-Bit Data Transfer Algorithm

When sending a byte (e.g., the character 'A' = 0x41) to the LCD in 4-bit mode:

1. Extract the **upper nibble**: `(byte >> 4)` = 0x04.
2. Place upper nibble on data lines D4–D7.
3. Set **RS = 1** (data mode), **R/W = 0** (write).
4. Pulse **EN HIGH for ≥ 450 ns**, then pull LOW. LCD latches upper nibble.
5. Extract the **lower nibble**: `(byte & 0x0F)` = 0x01.
6. Place lower nibble on D4–D7.
7. Pulse **EN** again to latch the lower nibble.
8. Wait **37 µs** (or check busy flag) before next command.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Students often forget that in 4-bit mode, **every byte requires two EN pulses** — one for the upper nibble, one for the lower nibble. This is the #1 reason LCD initialization fails in student code.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question]**

**Q1. Differentiate between Common Anode and Common Cathode seven-segment displays. How is the segment code for digit '7' derived in each case?**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Common Anode (CA) | Common Cathode (CC) |
|---|---|---|
| Common pin connection | Tied to **+Vcc (+5V)** | Tied to **GND (0V)** |
| Segment ON condition | Apply **LOW** to segment pin | Apply **HIGH** to segment pin |
| Active logic level | Active LOW | Active HIGH |
| Current flow direction | MCU pin **sinks** current | MCU pin **sources** current |

**Derivation for digit '7':** The digit 7 lights segments **a, b, c** (top + top-right + bottom-right).

- **Common Cathode code:** 0b01110000 → **0x70** (with dp=0)
- **Common Anode code:** Bitwise NOT of CC code → 0b10001111 → **0x8F**

---

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**Q2. What is switch bouncing? Explain software debouncing with a flowchart.**

**Model Answer:**

**Switch Bouncing:** When a mechanical push-button is pressed or released, the metal contacts physically vibrate against each other for a short duration (typically **5 to 20 ms**), producing multiple spurious ON-OFF transitions before settling to a stable state. If left unhandled, the microcontroller interprets these bounces as multiple valid key presses, leading to incorrect counting or erroneous behavior.

**Software Debouncing Method:**

1. Detect first HIGH-to-LOW transition on the key pin.
2. Start a software timer (or use `delay_ms(20)`) to wait for bouncing to settle.
3. After 20 ms, re-read the pin. If still LOW, the press is **valid**.
4. Wait for the key to be released (pin returns HIGH) and apply the same 20 ms debounce delay on release.
5. Update the application counter/state only after the debounced press is confirmed.

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Module 2 Model Paper]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Seven Segment + LED**

**(a)** Interface an 8-LED array to **Port P1** of the 8051 microcontroller through appropriate current-limiting resistors. Write an 8051 C program to generate the following running light pattern: LEDs light up sequentially from P1.0 to P1.7, then reverse direction from P1.7 back to P1.0, with a 500 ms delay between each transition. Use **Timer 0 in Mode 1** for delay generation. Assume a crystal frequency of **11.0592 MHz**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Interface a **4-digit common-cathode seven-segment display** to the 8051. Write the complete C program to display a **4-digit BCD counter from 0000 to 9999** that increments every 1 second. Implement display multiplexing using Timer 0 interrupt. Show the segment code lookup table for all digits 0–9. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q1(a):**

**Hardware Connection:** Each LED's anode is connected to P1.0–P1.7 via a 220 Ω current-limiting resistor; all LED cathodes are tied to GND. Port P1 is configured as output (default in 8051 quasi-bidirectional mode).

**Timer Calculation for 500 ms:**

$$
T_{machine} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \text{ MHz}} = 1.085 \text{ µs}
$$

Number of machine cycles for 500 ms:

$$
N_{cycles} = \frac{500{,}000 \text{ µs}}{1.085 \text{ µs}} \approx 460{,}829
$$

Since 460,829 > 65,536 (max 16-bit timer count), we cannot generate 500 ms in a single timer overflow. We use a **software counter variable**. Let the timer overflow every 50 ms and count 10 overflows.

Reload value for 50 ms:

$$
N_{50ms} = \frac{50{,}000}{1.085} \approx 46{,}083
$$

$$
Reload = 65536 - 46083 = 19453 = 0x4BFD
$$

TH0 = 0x4C, TL0 = 0xFD (accounting for 1-cycle adjustment).

**Complete 8051 C Program:**

```c
#include <reg51.h>

sbit LED_BUS = P1;   // 8-LED bank on Port 1
unsigned char pattern = 0x01;   // Initial: only P1.0 ON
unsigned char direction = 0;    // 0 = forward, 1 = reverse
unsigned int overflow_count = 0;

void timer0_isr(void) interrupt 1 {
    TH0 = 0x4C;
    TL0 = 0xFD;
    overflow_count++;
    if (overflow_count >= 10) {   // 10 × 50 ms = 500 ms
        overflow_count = 0;
        LED_BUS = pattern;        // Update LED pattern
        if (direction == 0) {
            pattern <<= 1;
            if (pattern == 0x00) { pattern = 0x40; direction = 1; }
        } else {
            pattern >>= 1;
            if (pattern == 0x00) { pattern = 0x02; direction = 0; }
        }
    }
}

void main(void) {
    TMOD = 0x01;      // Timer 0, Mode 1 (16-bit)
    TH0 = 0x4C;
    TL0 = 0xFD;
    ET0 = 1;          // Enable Timer 0 interrupt
    EA = 1;           // Enable global interrupt
    TR0 = 1;          // Start Timer 0
    while (1);        // Idle — work done in ISR
}
```

**Key Explanation:** The ISR is invoked every 50 ms; after 10 invocations (500 ms), the LED pattern is updated. The variable `direction` flips the light movement when the leading bit reaches the end. Using interrupts (vs. polled `delay()` loops) allows the CPU to perform other tasks concurrently — a fundamental KTU lab concept.

---

**Model Solution for Q1(b):**

**Hardware:**
- P0.0–P0.7 → segment lines a–g + dp (via 220 Ω resistors)
- P2.0–P2.3 → digit select lines (via BC547 NPN transistors to sink common cathodes)
- P3.4 → push button (optional, for manual increment)

**Segment Code Lookup Table (Common Cathode):**

```c
unsigned char code seg_code[10] = {
    0x3F,  // 0 → a,b,c,d,e,f ON
    0x06,  // 1 → b,c ON
    0x5B,  // 2 → a,b,d,e,g ON
    0x4F,  // 3 → a,b,c,d,g ON
    0x66,  // 4 → b,c,f,g ON
    0x6D,  // 5 → a,c,d,f,g ON
    0x7D,  // 6 → a,c,d,e,f,g ON
    0x07,  // 7 → a,b,c ON
    0x7F,  // 8 → all ON
    0x6F   // 9 → a,b,c,d,f,g ON
};
```

**Complete Program:**

```c
#include <reg51.h>

unsigned int count = 0;
unsigned char digit[4];

void update_digits(void) {
    digit[0] = count / 1000;        // Thousands
    digit[1] = (count / 100) % 10;  // Hundreds
    digit[2] = (count / 10) % 10;   // Tens
    digit[3] = count % 10;          // Units
}

void timer0_isr(void) interrupt 1 {
    static unsigned char d = 0;
    static unsigned int sec_count = 0;
    TH0 = 0x4C;
    TL0 = 0xFD;
    P2 = 0xFF;                       // De-select all digits
    P0 = seg_code[digit[d]];         // Output segment code
    P2 = ~(0x01 << d);               // Select current digit
    d = (d + 1) % 4;
    if (++sec_count >= 20) {         // 20 × 50 ms = 1000 ms
        sec_count = 0;
        count = (count + 1) % 10000;
        update_digits();
    }
}

void main(void) {
    unsigned char code seg_code[10] = {0x3F,0x06,0x5B,0x4F,0x66,0x6D,0x7D,0x07,0x7F,0x6F};
    update_digits();
    TMOD = 0x01;
    TH0 = 0x4C;
    TL0 = 0xFD;
    ET0 = 1; EA = 1; TR0 = 1;
    while (1);
}
```

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — LCD + Matrix Keypad**

**(a)** Interface a **16×2 LCD in 4-bit mode** to the 8051. The connections are: **P3.0 = RS, P3.1 = R/W, P3.2 = EN, P3.4–P3.7 = LCD data D4–D7**. Write a complete C program to display the message **"KTU 2024 LAB"** on the first line starting from column 3, and **"MICRO LAB"** on the second line starting from column 4. Show the LCD initialization sequence clearly. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Interface a **4×4 matrix keypad** to Port P1 (rows: P1.0–P1.3, columns: P1.4–P1.7) of the 8051. Write a C program that detects a key press and displays the corresponding key value on the LCD from part (a). Implement **software debouncing of 20 ms** and ensure that only **one valid press** is registered per physical key press. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q2(a):**

```c
#include <reg51.h>

sbit RS = P3^0;
sbit RW = P3^1;
sbit EN = P3^2;
#define LCD_DATA P3

void lcd_pulse(void) {
    EN = 1;
    _nop_(); _nop_();
    EN = 0;
    _nop_(); _nop_();
}

void lcd_write(unsigned char cmd, unsigned char mode) {
    RS = mode;       // 0 = command, 1 = data
    RW = 0;
    LCD_DATA = (LCD_DATA & 0x0F) | (cmd & 0xF0);   // Upper nibble
    lcd_pulse();
    LCD_DATA = (LCD_DATA & 0x0F) | ((cmd << 4) & 0xF0);  // Lower nibble
    lcd_pulse();
    if (cmd == 0x01 || cmd == 0x02) _delay_ms(2);
    else _delay_us(40);
}

void lcd_init(void) {
    _delay_ms(20);
    lcd_write(0x33, 0);
    _delay_ms(5);
    lcd_write(0x32, 0);
    _delay_us(100);
    lcd_write(0x28, 0);  // 4-bit, 2-line, 5x7
    lcd_write(0x0C, 0);  // Display ON, cursor OFF
    lcd_write(0x01, 0);  // Clear display
    lcd_write(0x06, 0);  // Entry mode
}

void lcd_string(char *str) {
    while (*str) lcd_write(*str++, 1);
}

void main(void) {
    lcd_init();
    lcd_write(0x83, 0);             // Line 1, Column 3
    lcd_string("KTU 2024 LAB");
    lcd_write(0xC4, 0);             // Line 2, Column 4
    lcd_string("MICRO LAB");
    while (1);
}
```

---

**Model Solution for Q2(b):**

```c
#include <reg51.h>
#include "lcd.h"   // Include the LCD routines from Q2(a)

unsigned char keypad[4][4] = {
    {'7','8','9','/'},
    {'4','5','6','*'},
    {'1','2','3','-'},
    {'C','0','=','+'}
};

unsigned char scan_keypad(void) {
    unsigned char row, col;
    for (row = 0; row < 4; row++) {
        P1 = ~(0x01 << row);   // Drive current row LOW
        for (col = 0; col < 4; col++) {
            if (!(P1 & (0x10 << col))) {    // Column pin LOW = key pressed
                _delay_ms(20);              // Debounce
                if (!(P1 & (0x10 << col))) {
                    while (!(P1 & (0x10 << col)));  // Wait for release
                    _delay_ms(20);          // Debounce release
                    return keypad[row][col];
                }
            }
        }
    }
    return 0;   // No key pressed
}

void main(void) {
    unsigned char key;
    lcd_init();
    lcd_write(0x80, 0);
    lcd_string("Key: ");
    P1 = 0xF0;   // Rows LOW, Columns HIGH initially
    while (1) {
        key = scan_keypad();
        if (key != 0) {
            lcd_write(0x85, 0);   // Position cursor after "Key: "
            lcd_write(key, 1);    // Display the key
        }
    }
}
```

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Marks Losers:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the 1.53 ms delay** after sending the LCD clear command (0x01) — costs **2 marks**.
> 2. **Wrong segment code for digit 0** — students often code 0x3F incorrectly as 0x3E (forgetting segment 'a'). Always cross-verify with a segment table.
> 3. **Not enabling internal pull-ups** on keypad column pins — when the key is released, the pin floats and reads random values. Use external 10 kΩ pull-ups to Vcc.
> 4. **Using `_delay_ms()` in an ISR** — this blocks higher-priority interrupts. Use software counters instead, as shown in Q1(a).
> 5. **Confusing active-LOW vs active-HIGH** for the common pin in SSDs. Always state explicitly whether the design uses CA or CC and provide the corresponding lookup table.
> 6. **Missing the `EA = 1` global interrupt enable** — the most common oversight in 8051 interrupt programs. Without it, no interrupt is serviced.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2**

- ☐ **LED interfacing requires a current-limiting resistor**, sized using $R = (V_{cc} - V_f)/I_f$. Standard value: **220 Ω** for 5V systems.
- ☐ **8051 P1, P2, P3** are quasi-bidirectional; **P0** is open-drain and requires external pull-ups.
- ☐ **Common Cathode SSD**: Active HIGH segments. **Common Anode SSD**: Active LOW segments (invert CC code).
- ☐ **Multiplexing** reduces pin count: N digits share 8 segment lines and use N common pins. Maintain refresh rate **> 60 Hz**, preferably **200 Hz**.
- ☐ **HD44780 LCD** uses ASCII codes for data and command codes for control. The **clear (0x01)** and **home (0x02)** commands require **1.53 ms** delay.
- ☐ **4-bit LCD mode** requires **two EN pulses** per byte (upper nibble first, then lower nibble).
- ☐ **LCD initialization sequence**: Wait 15 ms → `0x03` three times → `0x02` → `0x28` → `0x0C` → `0x01` → `0x06`.
- ☐ **Matrix keypad scanning** can be done by row or column; both are algorithmically equivalent. **Always use 20 ms debounce.**
- ☐ **Switch bouncing** is a mechanical phenomenon (5–20 ms) causing multiple false triggers. Handle it via **software delay** or **hardware RC filter + Schmitt trigger (74HC14)**.
- ☐ **Timer 0 Mode 1** is 16-bit. Reload formula: `TH0:TL0 = 65536 - (delay_us / 1.085)`.
- ☐ **Interrupt-driven design** (vs polled loops) is preferred in KTU lab exams because it allows concurrent task execution and demonstrates advanced understanding.
- ☐ **Busy flag (BF)** polling on DB7 is a more robust alternative to fixed delay timing for LCD communication.
- ☐ **Proteus ISIS** is the standard KTU simulation environment. Learn to use the **logic analyzer** and **virtual terminal** tools for debugging.
- ☐ **Viva-favorite question**: *"Can we interface an LCD using only 6 pins?"* — **Yes**, by tying R/W permanently to GND (write-only mode) and using 4-bit data mode.
- ☐ **Viva-favorite question**: *"What is the role of the contrast pin (VEE) on the LCD?"* — It adjusts the LCD's viewing angle by controlling the voltage applied to the liquid crystal segment electrodes. Typically fed through a 10 kΩ potentiometer between Vcc and GND.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ADC Interface
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCECL408 - MICROCONTROLLERS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** ADC Interface # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition An **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** is an embedded electronic subsystem that samples a continuous-time, continuous-amplitude analog input signal and converts it into a discrete-time, discrete-amplitude digital codeword — typically a binary integer — that a microcontroller can store, process, and transmit. In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme Microcontrollers Lab, *ADC Interface* refers to the systematic hardware bus and firmware mechanism by which an external (e.g., ADC0808/0809) or on-chip (e.g., LPC2148's 10-bit SAR ADC) converter is electrically and logically coupled to the host microcontroller so that real-world analog quantities (temperature, light, pressure, potentiometer wiper voltage) become readable digital variables inside a C/Embedded-C program. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** *ADC Interface* under **PCECL408 – Module Experiment 2** expects the student to demonstrate (a) correct hardware pin mapping between the ADC and the microcontroller port pins, (b) accurate timing-diagram compliant control of the ADC's start-of-conversion and end-of-conversion handshake signals, and (c) correct interpretation of the binary codeword back into engineering units. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition Imagine you are standing at the seashore with a ruler marked only in whole meters. The ocean's tide is rising continuously — it has an *infinite* number of water levels between 0 m and 5 m. You cannot measure or record every possible level, so you **snap a photograph of the tide once every hour** (this is *sampling*) and in each photograph you round the level to the nearest whole meter on your ruler (this is *quantization*). At the end of the day you have 24 numbers, each an integer from 0 to 5, that represent the day's tide. The ocean = analog signal, your hourly snapshot = sampling, the integer meter reading = quantization, and the photo album = the digital codeword stored in the microcontroller. **That is exactly what an ADC does — but it does it millions of times per second, and with much finer gradations (8-bit, 10-bit, 12-bit, …).** > [!NOTE] > **Two Pillars of ADC Behaviour** > 1. **Sampling** — captures the signal's instantaneous amplitude at discrete time instants (governed by the *sampling theorem*). > 2. **Quantization** — rounds the sampled amplitude to the nearest representable digital level (governed by *resolution*). ## 1.3 Key Vocabulary and Standard Metrics - **Resolution (n):** The number of bits in the digital output codeword. KTU boards typically use **8-bit ADC0808/0809** (256 levels) or **10-bit on-chip ADC of LPC2148** (1024 levels). - **Step Size (ΔV):** The smallest analog voltage difference the ADC can distinguish. - **Reference Voltage (V_ref):** The highest analog voltage that produces the full-scale digital output codeword. - **Conversion Time (t_conv):** The wall-clock time the ADC needs to complete one conversion cycle (e.g., ~100 µs for ADC0808). - **Throughput / Sampling Rate (f_s):** The number of conversions completed per second (e.g., ADC0808 ≤ **10 kSPS**). - **Quantization Error (e_q):** The maximum inherent rounding error in a single conversion. > [!TIP] > All five of these parameters (**resolution**, **step size**, **V_ref**, **conversion time**, and **quantization error**) are favourite KTU viva-voice and 3-mark question topics. Memorise their symbol, definition, and unit in one sitting. --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Logic of a Successive-Approximation ADC (Most Common in KTU Boards) The **Successive Approximation Register (SAR) ADC** is the dominant architecture used in microcontrollers (LPC2148, STM32, MSP430) and in the classic ADC0808/0809 IC. Its logic proceeds as follows: 1. The **Sample-and-Hold (S/H)** circuit charges an internal capacitor array to track the input voltage V_in. 2. The SAR register sets its most-significant bit (MSB) to 1 and all other bits to 0 — i.e., it tries *half* of V_ref. 3. An internal precision comparator decides whether V_in is **greater than** or **less than** this trial code. 4. If V_in > trial value → MSB is **kept at 1**; otherwise MSB is **reset to 0**. 5. The algorithm then repeats with the next bit, narrowing the search window by half each step. 6. After *n* comparisons (one per output bit), the SAR contains the final *n*-bit digital code. > [!IMPORTANT] > The **why** behind the elegance: each comparison halves the uncertainty, so for an *n*-bit ADC exactly **n clock cycles** are needed. This gives SAR ADCs a constant, predictable conversion time — a property real-time embedded firmware can rely on. ## 2.2 Why "Why" Matters — Engineering Utility - **Closed-loop motor control** requires sampling current/voltage at ≥ 10 kHz → SAR's deterministic latency is mandatory. - **Audio digitisation** requires ≥ 44.1 kSPS and ≥ 16-bit resolution → SAR/Σ-Δ ADCs are used. - **Battery monitoring** requires only ~1 kSPS and 10–12-bit resolution → ultra-low-power SAR is ideal. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula / Definition | Unit | Boundary / Range | |---|----------|----------------------|------|------------------| | 1 | Digital output codeword (D) | $D = \left\lfloor \dfrac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \times 2^{n} \right\rfloor$ | dimensionless integer | $0 \le D \le 2^{n}-1$ | | 2 | Step size (ΔV) | $\Delta V = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^{n}}$ | Volts (V) | $2^{n}$ steps cover 0 → V_ref | | 3 | Quantization error (e_q) | $e_{q} \le \pm \dfrac{\Delta V}{2}$ | Volts (V) | Uniformly distributed in $[-\Delta V/2, +\Delta V/2]$ | | 4 | Reconstructed analog value (V_in) | $V_{in} = D \cdot \Delta V$ | Volts (V) | Subject to ±ΔV/2 uncertainty | | 5 | Full-scale voltage (V_FS) | $V_{FS} = V_{ref} \cdot \dfrac{2^{n}-1}{2^{n}} \approx V_{ref}$ | Volts (V) | Maximum *non-saturating* input | | 6 | Nyquist minimum sampling rate | $f_{s} \ge 2 \cdot f_{max}$ | Samples/second (SPS) | $f_{max}$ = highest signal frequency component | | 7 | Conversion time (SAR, n-bit) | $t_{conv} = n \cdot T_{CLK}$ | seconds | $T_{CLK}$ = ADC clock period | | 8 | Throughput (max conversion rate) | $f_{s,max} = \dfrac{1}{t_{conv}}$ | SPS | Inverse of conversion time | | 9 | ADC0808 t_conv (typical) | $\approx \mathbf{100\ \mu s}$ | seconds | At 640 kHz clock | | 10 | Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio (SQNR) | $SQNR_{dB} \approx 6.02 \cdot n + 1.76$ | dB | For ideal n-bit ADC | > [!CAUTION] > Never write absolute-value bars `|x|` inside a markdown table — the pipe character breaks the table parser. In your **exam answer sheet** absolute-value bars are perfectly fine in plain handwriting/typed math outside tables. ## 2.4 Pin-Out and Signal Roles of ADC0808/0809 (High-Yield for Lab) | Pin | Symbol | Role in Interfacing | |-----|--------|---------------------| | 1–8, 26–28 | IN0–IN7 | 8 analog input channels (single-ended) | | 25 | A, B, C | Channel-select address lines (latched by ALE) | | 23 | ALE | Address Latch Enable — latches A, B, C | | 22 | START | Rising-edge-triggered Start-of-Conversion | | 7 | EOC | End-of-Conversion (goes LOW when conversion done) | | 9 | OE | Output Enable — tri-states digital outputs | | 11–18 | D7–D0 | 8-bit parallel digital output bus | | 12, 16 | V_ref+, V_ref− | Reference voltages (set step size) | | 13 | CLK | External conversion clock (typ. 640 kHz) | | 10, 15 | V_CC, GND | Power and ground | > [!NOTE] > In Lab Record + Viva, students are expected to memorise **START, EOC, OE, ALE, CLK, V_ref+**, and **V_ref−** in particular. These are the 7 pins almost always asked in oral examinations. --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Digital Output Codeword from V_in We want to derive the exact relationship between an analog input voltage $V_{in}$ and the resulting digital output codeword $D$ of an ideal $n$-bit ADC. **Step 1 — Define the Full-Scale Range.** By construction, an $n$-bit ADC represents voltages in the range $[0,\ V_{ref}]$ using $2^{n}$ equally-spaced discrete levels (from code 0 to code $2^{n}-1$). $$ \text{Number of codes} = 2^{n} $$ **Step 2 — Compute the Step Size.** The width of one code level (one LSB) is the full-scale range divided by the number of codes: $$ \Delta V \;=\; \frac{V_{ref}}{2^{n}} $$ **Step 3 — Express V_in as a Multiple of Step Size.** For any analog input, the *ideal* digital codeword is the integer number of steps that fit between 0 V and $V_{in}$: $$ D \;=\; \frac{V_{in}}{\Delta V} \;=\; \frac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}\,/\,2^{n}} \;=\; \frac{V_{in} \cdot 2^{n}}{V_{ref}} $$ **Step 4 — Apply the Floor Function (Quantisation).** Because the ADC cannot output fractions, we must *truncate to the nearest integer*: $$ \boxed{\,D \;=\; \left\lfloor \frac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \cdot 2^{n} \right\rfloor\,} $$ **Step 5 — Sanity Checks.** - If $V_{in} = 0$ → $D = 0$ ✓ (no signal, no code). - If $V_{in} = V_{ref}$ → $D = 2^{n}$, but the maximum *representable* code is $2^{n}-1$ (one LSB below full scale). - Therefore the practical saturation voltage is $V_{FS} = V_{ref} \cdot (2^{n}-1)/2^{n}$. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Maximum Quantisation Error **Step 1 — Worst-Case Assumption.** A true analog voltage $V_{in}$ can lie anywhere inside one quantisation interval of width $\Delta V$. The chosen code places it at the *centre* of that interval. **Step 2 — Maximum Distance from Centre to Edge.** The maximum distance from the centre of an interval to its edge is $\Delta V / 2$. **Step 3 — State the Bound.** $$ e_{q} \;=\; \vert V_{in} - D \cdot \Delta V \vert \;\le\; \frac{\Delta V}{2} $$ **Step 4 — Substitute ΔV.** $$ \boxed{\,e_{q,\max} \;=\; \frac{V_{ref}}{2^{n+1}}\,} $$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) > **Problem:** An **8-bit ADC0808** is connected with $V_{ref+} = +5.00$ V and $V_{ref-} = 0$ V. Compute the digital output when the analog input at channel IN3 is **$V_{in} = 2.40$ V**. Then compute (i) the step size, (ii) the quantisation error, and (iii) the back-calculated analog voltage. **Step 1 — Identify parameters.** $$ n = 8,\qquad V_{ref} = 5.00\ \text{V},\qquad V_{in} = 2.40\ \text{V} $$ **Step 2 — Compute step size.** $$ \Delta V \;=\; \frac{V_{ref}}{2^{n}} \;=\; \frac{5.00}{2^{8}} \;=\; \frac{5.00}{256} \;\approx\; 0.01953\ \text{V} \;=\; 19.53\ \text{mV} $$ **Step 3 — Compute ideal digital codeword.** $$ D_{\text{ideal}} \;=\; \frac{V_{in} \cdot 2^{n}}{V_{ref}} \;=\; \frac{2.40 \times 256}{5.00} \;=\; \frac{614.4}{5.00} \;=\; 122.88 $$ **Step 4 — Apply floor (quantisation).** $$ D \;=\; \lfloor 122.88 \rfloor \;=\; 122_{10} $$ **Step 5 — Convert to 8-bit binary (handy for hardware interface code).** $$ 122_{10} \;=\; 0111\,1010_{2} \;=\; \text{0x7A} $$ So the firmware would read the value `0x7A` (or `122`) from the ADC's data port. **Step 6 — Back-calculate the reconstructed analog voltage.** $$ V_{\text{reconstructed}} \;=\; D \cdot \Delta V \;=\; 122 \times 0.01953 \;\approx\; 2.383\ \text{V} $$ **Step 7 — Compute quantisation error.** $$ e_{q} \;=\; \vert V_{in} - V_{\text{reconstructed}} \vert \;=\; \vert 2.40 - 2.383 \vert \;\approx\; 0.017\ \text{V} $$ This is well within the theoretical bound $\Delta V/2 \approx 9.77$ mV → ✓ consistent. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Decision Table for KTU Lab Viva | Question Asked in Viva | One-Line Correct Answer | |------------------------|--------------------------| | Why does ADC0808 need an external clock? | Internal SAR requires a stable conversion clock (typ. 500 kHz – 640 kHz); it has no internal oscillator. | | Why is EOC active-LOW? | Convention to indicate "busy" status: LOW ⇒ conversion in progress or just completed, HIGH ⇒ ready for next start. | | Can V_ref+ exceed V_CC? | No. V_ref+ ≤ V_CC to avoid latch-up; absolute max = 6.5 V for ADC0808. | | Difference between ADC0808 and ADC0809? | ADC0808 = 8-bit with **8 channels**; ADC0809 is the same die with minor timing/speed grade differences. Functionally interchangeable in lab. | | Which bit of 8-bit code is MSB? | D7 (pin 18 of ADC0808). | | Sampling theorem requirement? | $f_s \ge 2 f_{max}$ (Nyquist criterion) to avoid aliasing. | --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 System-Level Block Diagram of ADC0808 ↔ Microcontroller Interface ```mermaid flowchart LR analogSrc["Analog Source
Sensor or POT"] --> adcIn["ADC0808
IN0 to IN7"] adcIn --> sHold["Sample and Hold
Internal Capacitor"] sHold --> sarReg["SAR + Comparator
8-bit Register"] clkSrc["External Clock
640 kHz Oscillator"] --> clkPin["ADC CLK Pin 10"] clkPin --> sarReg addrLines["Address Lines A B C
from MC Port Pins"] --> alePin["ALE Pin 23"] alePin --> channelLatch["Channel Latch"] channelLatch --> adcIn startPin["START Pulse Pin 22
from MC"] --> sarReg sarReg --> eocPin["EOC Pin 7
Falling Edge Flag"] eocPin --> mcInt["MC INT or Polled Pin"] oePin["OE Pin 9
from MC"] --> triState["Tri-State Buffers"] sarReg --> triState triState --> dataBus["8-bit Data Bus D0 to D7
to MC Port"] vrefTop["V ref plus 5V"] --> adcIn vrefBot["V ref minus 0V GND"] --> adcIn ``` > [!NOTE] > The diagram above maps every KTU-relevant ADC0808 pin to its functional role and shows the bidirectional information flow between analog domain (left), ADC internals (centre), and digital microcontroller bus (right). Students can redraw this for the **Lab Record File** with exact pin numbers. ## 4.2 Firmware Flow-Chart — Embedded C ADC Read Routine ```mermaid flowchart TD stepA["Step A: Initialize MC Ports
Set address lines A B C as OUTPUT"] stepB["Step B: Configure OE as OUTPUT
Set OE HIGH initially"] stepC["Step C: Configure EOC as INPUT"] stepD["Step D: Select channel
Write A B C bits to port"] stepE["Step E: Pulse ALE HIGH then LOW
to latch channel address"] stepF["Step F: Pulse START HIGH then LOW
begin conversion"] stepG["Step G: Poll EOC pin
wait for LOW to HIGH transition"] stepH["Step H: Drive OE HIGH
enables tri-state output buffers"] stepI["Step I: Read 8-bit data bus
store into variable ADC value"] stepJ["Step J: Drive OE LOW
disable tri-state"] stepK["Step K: Return ADC value
to calling function"] stepA --> stepB --> stepC --> stepD --> stepE --> stepF --> stepG --> stepH --> stepI --> stepJ --> stepK ``` ## 4.3 SAR Conversion Sequence (Internal Algorithm Visualisation) ```mermaid flowchart LR msbTrial["Trial 1000 0000
Compare with V ref by 2"] msbTrial --> msbDecision{Is V in greater than trial} msbDecision -- YES --> keepMsb1["Keep MSB as 1"] msbDecision -- NO --> clearMsb0["Clear MSB to 0"] keepMsb1 --> bit6Trial["Trial Bit 6 with remaining range by 2"] clearMsb0 --> bit6Trial bit6Trial --> bit5Trial["Trial Bit 5"] bit5Trial --> bit4Trial["Trial Bit 4"] bit4Trial --> bit3Trial["Trial Bit 3"] bit3Trial --> bit2Trial["Trial Bit 2"] bit2Trial --> bit1Trial["Trial Bit 1"] bit1Trial --> bit0Trial["Trial Bit 0"] bit0Trial --> finalCode["Final 8-bit codeword D7 to D0"] ``` > [!TIP] > Always narrate the SAR diagram in your lab record with the sentence: *"Each comparison halves the remaining voltage window, so an n-bit conversion completes in exactly n clock cycles."* Examiners award full marks when this phrase appears verbatim. --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` Define *resolution* and *step size* of an ADC. An 8-bit ADC has a reference voltage of 5 V. Compute its step size and full-scale output code. **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Resolution** of an ADC is the smallest change in analog input voltage that can be detected, determined by the number of bits *n* in its output. It is often expressed as the number of discrete levels, i.e., $2^{n}$. > **Step size (ΔV)** is the analog voltage represented by one LSB increment of the digital code. $$ \Delta V \;=\; \frac{V_{ref}}{2^{n}} \;=\; \frac{5\ \text{V}}{2^{8}} \;=\; \frac{5}{256} \;\approx\; 19.53\ \text{mV} $$ > **Full-scale output code** = $2^{n}-1 = 255_{10} = \text{0xFF} = 1111\,1111_{2}$, occurring when $V_{in} \approx V_{ref}$. ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` What is the role of the EOC pin of ADC0808? Why is it necessary to poll it before reading the digital output? **Model Answer (3 marks):** > The **End-of-Conversion (EOC)** pin signals the *completion status* of the ongoing analog-to-digital conversion. It transitions from **HIGH to LOW** at the start of conversion (busy) and back to **HIGH** when the conversion finishes and the result is latched in the internal output register. > Polling EOC is necessary because if the microcontroller reads the data bus **before** EOC goes HIGH, the value retrieved will be either the *previous* code or a *garbled mid-conversion* code. Hence the firmware must: > 1. Issue a START pulse, > 2. Wait until EOC returns HIGH (or wait on an interrupt triggered by EOC's rising edge), > 3. Then assert OE to read the stable 8-bit result. > This handshake ensures **data integrity**, a 3-mark-worthy phrase. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each) ### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024]` > **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the interfacing of **ADC0808** with the **8051 microcontroller**. Show the connections of address lines, START, EOC, OE, and data bus. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** An **8-bit ADC** has $V_{ref} = 5.12$ V. Compute (i) step size, (ii) digital output for $V_{in} = 3.20$ V, (iii) quantisation error, and (iv) the back-calculated analog value. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for Q.A(a): **Block Diagram Description:** The 8051 microcontroller (Port 0 used as bidirectional data bus, Port 2 used for control signals) is interfaced to the ADC0808 as follows: 1. **Analog Inputs IN0–IN7** of ADC0808 are connected to the analog sources (e.g., potentiometer, LM35 temperature sensor). 2. **Address lines A, B, C** of ADC0808 are connected to three pins of Port 1 (e.g., P1.0, P1.1, P1.2). To select channel 3, the firmware outputs `011` on these pins. 3. **ALE pin** of ADC0808 is driven by a Port 1 pin (e.g., P1.3). The firmware pulses ALE HIGH→LOW to latch the channel address internally. 4. **START pin** of ADC0808 is driven by a Port 1 pin (e.g., P1.4). The firmware pulses START HIGH→LOW to begin conversion. 5. **EOC pin** of ADC0808 is connected to a Port 3 pin (e.g., P3.0 or to external interrupt INT0 = P3.2). The firmware polls P3.0 for a HIGH level. 6. **OE pin** of ADC0808 is driven by a Port 1 pin (e.g., P1.5). The firmware asserts OE HIGH to tri-state-enable the output buffers. 7. **D0–D7** of ADC0808 are connected to Port 0 of 8051 (with external pull-ups, since Port 0 has no internal pull-ups). 8. **CLK pin** of ADC0808 is fed from the 8051's ALE output divided by a suitable flip-flop/divider, or from a 640 kHz external oscillator. 9. **V_ref+ = 5.12 V, V_ref− = GND.** **Working Sequence (1 mark for each major step):** 1. Initialise ports: P1 = outputs for control, P3.0 = input for EOC. 2. Select channel: write channel number on A, B, C lines. 3. Pulse ALE HIGH then LOW. 4. Pulse START HIGH then LOW. 5. Wait for EOC = HIGH (poll or use interrupt). 6. Drive OE HIGH. 7. Read Port 0 into variable `adc_value`. 8. Drive OE LOW. 9. Convert `adc_value` to engineering units: `V_in = adc_value × 5.12 / 256`. #### Model Solution for Q.A(b): **Given:** $n = 8$, $V_{ref} = 5.12$ V, $V_{in} = 3.20$ V. **(i) Step size:** $$ \Delta V \;=\; \frac{V_{ref}}{2^{n}} \;=\; \frac{5.12}{256} \;=\; 0.02\ \text{V} \;=\; 20\ \text{mV} $$ **(ii) Digital output codeword:** $$ D \;=\; \left\lfloor \frac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \cdot 2^{n} \right\rfloor \;=\; \left\lfloor \frac{3.20 \times 256}{5.12} \right\rfloor \;=\; \left\lfloor 160 \right\rfloor \;=\; 160_{10} $$ In binary: $160_{10} = 1010\,0000_{2} = \text{0xA0}$. **(iii) Quantisation error:** $$ V_{\text{reconstructed}} \;=\; D \cdot \Delta V \;=\; 160 \times 0.02 \;=\; 3.20\ \text{V} $$ $$ e_{q} \;=\; \vert V_{in} - V_{\text{reconstructed}} \vert \;=\; \vert 3.20 - 3.20 \vert \;=\; 0\ \text{V} $$ (The error is exactly zero in this ideal case because 3.20 V is an exact multiple of ΔV.) **(iv) Back-calculated analog value:** 3.20 V (same as input, since the error is zero). --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` *(Internal-choice alternative)* > **(a)** Explain the successive-approximation technique of ADC with a neat flowchart. Why is it the most popular ADC architecture for microcontroller applications? **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** A 10-bit ADC has $V_{ref+} = 3.3$ V and $V_{ref-} = 0$ V. Calculate (i) the step size in mV, (ii) the digital output for $V_{in} = 1.65$ V, and (iii) the maximum quantisation error in mV. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for Q.B(a): **Successive Approximation Technique — Step-by-step logic (one mark per correct point):** 1. A sample-and-hold (S/H) circuit first captures and holds the input voltage $V_{in}$ stable throughout the conversion. 2. The successive approximation register (SAR) is a state machine that initially sets only its **MSB = 1**, all other bits = 0. This represents a trial voltage $V_{trial} = V_{ref} / 2$. 3. An internal **precision comparator** checks whether $V_{in} > V_{trial}$ or $V_{in} < V_{trial}$. 4. If $V_{in} > V_{trial}$, the MSB is **kept at 1**; otherwise, it is **reset to 0**. 5. The algorithm moves to the next bit (MSB-1), setting it to 1 and repeating the comparison. Each comparison narrows the uncertainty by a factor of 2. 6. After *n* such trials, the SAR contains the final *n*-bit digital code. **Flowchart of operation:** ``` BEGIN conversion │ ▼ Hold V_in on S/H capacitor │ ▼ For bit i = MSB down to LSB: Set bit i = 1, all lower bits = 0 Compare V_trial with V_in If V_in >= V_trial → keep bit i = 1 Else → reset bit i = 0 │ ▼ Output SAR contents as final code │ ▼ END conversion ``` **Why SAR is the most popular (3 marks worth of reasons):** - **Deterministic latency:** exactly $n$ clock cycles per conversion — easy for real-time firmware. - **Moderate resolution (8–18 bits) and moderate speed (≤ 10 MSPS)** — ideal sweet-spot for embedded sensing. - **Low power consumption** compared to flash ADCs. - **Compact silicon area** — easily integrated on-chip in microcontrollers (LPC2148, STM32, MSP430). - **No external sample capacitor required** — internal S/H sufficient for most embedded applications. #### Model Solution for Q.B(b): **Given:** $n = 10$, $V_{ref} = 3.3$ V, $V_{in} = 1.65$ V. **(i) Step size in mV:** $$ \Delta V \;=\; \frac{V_{ref}}{2^{n}} \;=\; \frac{3.3\ \text{V}}{1024} \;\approx\; 0.003223\ \text{V} \;=\; 3.223\ \text{mV} $$ **(ii) Digital output for $V_{in} = 1.65$ V:** $$ D \;=\; \left\lfloor \frac{1.65 \times 1024}{3.3} \right\rfloor \;=\; \left\lfloor 512 \right\rfloor \;=\; 512_{10} $$ In binary: $512_{10} = 10\,0000\,0000_{2}$. Note that $V_{in} = V_{ref}/2$ produces exactly half-scale code, as expected for a bipolar-free, rail-to-ground ADC. **(iii) Maximum quantisation error in mV:** $$ e_{q,\max} \;=\; \frac{\Delta V}{2} \;=\; \frac{3.223}{2} \;\approx\; 1.61\ \text{mV} $$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks** > 1. **Forgetting the floor function** when computing $D$ from $V_{in}$. Write $\lfloor ... \rfloor$ explicitly or say *"truncate to the nearest integer"*. **(−1 mark)** > 2. **Confusing V_ref and V_CC.** $V_{ref}$ is the *reference voltage* that defines full-scale, **not** the supply voltage. Many students write $\Delta V = V_{CC}/2^{n}$ — wrong if $V_{ref} \ne V_{CC}$. **(−2 marks)** > 3. **Mixing up START and OE timing.** START must be pulsed **before** waiting for EOC; OE must be pulsed **after** EOC goes HIGH. Reversing the order reads invalid data. **(−2 marks)** > 4. **Omitting units in numerical answers** (mV vs V). Always write the unit, especially in the step-size calculation. **(−1 mark)** > 5. **Forgetting to mention the Nyquist criterion** when explaining sampling rate selection. Always pair *sampling rate* with the words *"must be at least twice the highest signal frequency to avoid aliasing"*. **(−1 mark)** > 6. **Drawing block diagrams without pin numbers.** KTU examiners award only partial credit for unlabelled diagrams. Always label at least **D0–D7, EOC, START, OE, ALE, CLK, V_ref+**. **(−2 marks)** --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - **ADC** converts a continuous analog voltage into a discrete digital codeword using two stages: **sampling** (in time) and **quantisation** (in amplitude). - The master formula to remember forever is: $$ D \;=\; \left\lfloor \dfrac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \cdot 2^{n} \right\rfloor,\qquad \Delta V \;=\; \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^{n}} $$ - The **most negative code** is `0` (at $V_{in} = 0$ V), and the **most positive code** is $2^{n}-1$ (just below $V_{ref}$). - **Quantisation error** is bounded by $\pm \Delta V/2$; the *RMS* quantisation noise is $\Delta V / \sqrt{12}$. - The **SNR of an ideal n-bit ADC** is approximately **6.02 n + 1.76 dB** — known as the *6-dB-per-bit rule*. +1 bit of resolution ≈ +6 dB of dynamic range. - **Sampling theorem:** $f_s \ge 2 f_{max}$ (Nyquist). Below Nyquist → **aliasing** (irreversible distortion). - **SAR ADC** dominates microcontrollers because conversion time = $n \times T_{CLK}$ (deterministic). - **ADC0808/0809** has 8 channels, needs external clock (~640 kHz), and uses 8-bit parallel output. - **EOC handshake** is mandatory — read EOC = HIGH before asserting OE; otherwise the read value is stale or invalid. - **Resolution vs Accuracy are NOT the same.** A 12-bit ADC can have only 8-bit accuracy due to noise, nonlinearity, and reference drift. - **V_ref stability is critical.** A noisy V_ref directly multiplies into every conversion. Always decouple V_ref pins with 0.1 µF + 10 µF capacitors to ground. - **Always oversample in software** for noisy sensors (e.g., take 16 readings and average) — improves effective resolution by ~½ bit per 4× oversampling. - **Common lab calculation shortcut:** if $V_{ref} = 5.12$ V and $n = 8$, then $\Delta V = 0.02$ V exactly → back-calculation is integer-clean (no rounding artefacts). Use this $V_{ref}$ whenever the KTU question allows free choice of reference. - **MSB** of an $n$-bit code from ADC0808 is **D7** (pin 18 of the IC). - **Pin to memorise for viva:** ALE (latch address), START (begin conversion), EOC (conversion done), OE (enable output), CLK (conversion clock), V_ref+ / V_ref− (define step size). - **Anti-pattern to avoid in lab record:** never write *"we read the ADC"* — always write *"we polled P3.0 until EOC went HIGH, then asserted P1.5 (OE) and read Port 0 into the variable `adc_val`."* Specificity earns marks.

topics.title: DAC Interface with Waveform Generation
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECL408 - MICROCONTROLLERS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
