> The above is **hardware-agnostic Embedded C**, not Python. It shows the algorithmic flow examiners look for in viva.

## 3.4 SPI Mode for SSD1306 OLED — Step-by-Step

| Step | Action | Required Signal |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Pull CS **LOW** to select OLED. | Active-low chip select. |
| 2 | Send **Control byte**: `0x00` for command, `0x40` for data. | D/C pin (some boards hardwire it). |
| 3 | Pulse SCK 8 times to clock the byte (MSB first). | CPOL=0, CPHA=0. |
| 4 | For commands, clock the next byte as command opcode (e.g., `0xAE` = display OFF, `0xD5` = set clock). | Followed by args. |
| 5 | For data, clock display RAM bytes (`0x40` stream mode). | Streams pixels. |
| 6 | Pull CS **HIGH** to release bus. | End of transaction. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why MSB-first?** SPI is by default MSB-first. Some MCUs allow LSB-first; the SSD1306 **only** supports MSB-first. Sending LSB-first will produce garbage characters.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 System-Level Block Diagram (I²C LCD Interfacing)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[MCU Master] -->|SDA P1.0| B[I2C Bus]
    A -->|SCL P1.1| B
    B -->|4.7k Pullup to VCC| C[Pullup Network]
    C --> D[PCF8574 I2C Expander]
    D -->|D4 D5 D6 D7| E[HD44780 LCD Controller]
    D -->|RS RW E BL| E
    E -->|16 character x 2 line DDRAM| F[16x2 LCD Display]
    G[10k Potentiometer] -->|Vo Contrast| E
    H[+5V Supply] --> A
    H --> D
    H --> E
```

## 4.2 I²C Transaction Flowchart (START to STOP)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeS[Start Condition] --> nodeA[Send 7bit Address 0x27]
    nodeA --> nodeR[Send R_W bit 0 for write]
    nodeR --> nodeACK1{Ack received?}
    nodeACK1 -->|No| nodeERR[Error: Check wiring and address]
    nodeERR --> nodeRST[Bus Reset and Retry]
    nodeACK1 -->|Yes| nodeCMD[Send Control Byte RS 0 for command 1 for data]
    nodeCMD --> nodeD1[Send High Nibble of LCD Byte]
    nodeD1 --> nodeEN1[Pulse Enable HIGH then LOW]
    nodeEN1 --> nodeD2[Send Low Nibble of LCD Byte]
    nodeD2 --> nodeEN2[Pulse Enable HIGH then LOW]
    nodeEN2 --> nodeMORE{More data to send?}
    nodeMORE -->|Yes| nodeCMD
    nodeMORE -->|No| nodeP[Stop Condition]
    nodeP --> nodeEND[End of Transaction]
    nodeRST --> nodeS
```

## 4.3 SPI OLED Transaction Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant MCU as MCU Master
    participant BUS as SPI Bus
    participant OLED as SSD1306 OLED Slave

    MCU->>BUS: Pull CS LOW
    Note over BUS: CS goes active low
    MCU->>BUS: Clock out Control Byte 0x00 for command
    BUS->>OLED: Receive command mode flag
    MCU->>BUS: Clock out Command Byte e.g. 0xAE for display OFF
    BUS->>OLED: Display OFF executed
    MCU->>BUS: Clock out Command Byte 0xD5
    MCU->>BUS: Clock out Argument Byte 0x80
    BUS->>OLED: Set display clock to default
    MCU->>BUS: Clock out Command Byte 0x8D for charge pump
    MCU->>BUS: Clock out Argument Byte 0x14
    BUS->>OLED: Enable charge pump
    MCU->>BUS: Clock out Command Byte 0xAF
    BUS->>OLED: Display ON
    MCU->>BUS: Pull CS HIGH
    Note over BUS: Transaction complete
```

## 4.4 HD44780 Initialization Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodePWR[Power On] --> nodeDLY1[Wait 15 ms]
    nodeDLY1 --> nodeFS1[Send Function Set 0x30]
    nodeFS1 --> nodeDLY2[Wait 4.1 ms]
    nodeDLY2 --> nodeFS2[Send Function Set 0x30]
    nodeFS2 --> nodeDLY3[Wait 100 us]
    nodeDLY3 --> nodeFS3[Send Function Set 0x30]
    nodeFS3 --> nodeFS4[Send 0x20 to enter 4bit mode]
    nodeFS4 --> nodeSET[Function Set 0x28]
    nodeSET --> nodeOFF[Display OFF 0x08]
    nodeOFF --> nodeCLR[Clear Display 0x01]
    nodeCLR --> nodeDLY4[Wait 1.64 ms]
    nodeDLY4 --> nodeENT[Entry Mode 0x06]
    nodeENT --> nodeON[Display ON Cursor OFF 0x0C]
    nodeON --> nodeRDY[LCD Ready for Characters]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**
> **Q1.** Differentiate between SPI and I²C communication protocols. List **two** advantages and **one** disadvantage of each when interfacing a 16×2 LCD to a microcontroller.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> | Parameter | SPI | I²C |
> |---|---|---|
> | Wires required | 4 (MOSI, MISO, SCK, SS) | 2 (SDA, SCL) |
> | Speed | Faster (up to 10 MHz) | Slower (max 400 kHz fast mode) |
> | Addressing | Hardware chip-select | Software 7-bit address |
> | Duplex | Full-duplex | Half-duplex |
> | **Advantage for LCD** | Faster refresh for graphical content. | Saves MCU pins; multiple devices on same bus. |
> | **Disadvantage for LCD** | Consumes 4 GPIO pins per slave. | Slower; needs pull-up resistors. |

> **`[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`**
> **Q2.** What is the function of the **PCF8574 IC** in an I²C LCD module? Mention the typical I²C address used.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The **PCF8574** is an **8-bit I²C-to-parallel port expander**. It acts as a bridge that converts the 2-wire I²C signals from the microcontroller into the 8 parallel control lines (RS, RW, E, BL, D4–D7) required by the **HD44780 LCD controller** operating in **4-bit mode**. The standard 7-bit slave address is **`0x27`** (or `0x3F`, depending on the variant — A0/A1/A2 pin strapping). The address byte transmitted is `0x27 << 1 = 0x4E` for write operations.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**
>
> **Question A (14 Marks):**
>
> **(a)** Draw the I²C bus interface circuit between an 8051 microcontroller and a 16×2 LCD using a PCF8574 expander. Label SDA, SCL, the pull-up resistors, and the contrast pot. State the standard 7-bit I²C address of the LCD backpack. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Write the **initialization sequence** (with correct command bytes) required to configure the HD44780 in 4-bit, 2-line, 5×8-font mode via I²C. Explain the role of the three "magic" `0x30` commands. **(7 marks)**
>
> ---
>
> **Model Solution:**
>
> **Part (a) — Circuit (4 marks for diagram + 3 marks for labels and address):**
>
> The diagram matches **Section 4.1** above. Expected labelled elements:
>
> 1. **8051 P1.0 → SDA** of PCF8574 (1 mark)
> 2. **8051 P1.1 → SCL** of PCF8574 (1 mark)
> 3. **4.7 kΩ pull-ups** on SDA and SCL to +5V (1 mark)
> 4. **10 kΩ potentiometer** on LCD pin 3 (Vo) for contrast (1 mark)
> 5. LCD data lines **D4–D7** connected to **P4–P7** of PCF8574 (1 mark)
> 6. **A0 = A1 = A2 = GND** to fix address (1 mark)
> 7. Standard 7-bit address: **`0x27`** (0x3F accepted as alternative) (1 mark)
>
> **Part (b) — Initialization Sequence (7 marks):**
>
> | Byte Order | Command (Hex) | Purpose | Marks |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | 1 | Wait ≥ 15 ms | Power-on stabilization | 1 |
> | 2 | `0x30` | 8-bit function set (1st) | 0.5 |
> | 3 | Wait ≥ 4.1 ms | | 0.5 |
> | 4 | `0x30` | 8-bit function set (2nd) | 0.5 |
> | 5 | Wait ≥ 100 µs | | 0.5 |
> | 6 | `0x30` | 8-bit function set (3rd) | 0.5 |
> | 7 | `0x20` | Switch to **4-bit mode** | 1 |
> | 8 | `0x28` | 4-bit, 2-line, 5×8 font | 0.5 |
> | 9 | `0x0C` | Display ON, cursor OFF | 0.5 |
> | 10 | `0x01` | Clear display (wait 1.64 ms) | 0.5 |
> | 11 | `0x06` | Entry mode: increment, no shift | 0.5 |
>
> **Role of the three 0x30 commands (1 mark):** They are sent in **8-bit mode** to safely reset the controller from any unknown state (caused by noisy power-up or previous data lines). The HD44780 reliably enters the known "8-bit, 1-line, 5×8" state after the third `0x30`, allowing the subsequent `0x20` to switch cleanly into 4-bit mode.

> **`[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`**
>
> **Question B (14 Marks):**
>
> **(a)** Explain the **four SPI modes** (Mode 0, 1, 2, 3) using CPOL and CPHA. State which mode the **SSD1306 OLED** uses and justify why this mode is required. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Write the step-by-step SPI command sequence (with hex opcodes) to **initialize and turn ON** a 128×64 SSD1306 OLED. Mention the role of the **charge pump** command. **(7 marks)**
>
> ---
>
> **Model Solution:**
>
> **Part (a) — SPI Modes (7 marks):**
>
> SPI timing is defined by two configuration bits:
>
> - **CPOL (Clock Polarity)** — defines the idle state of SCK.
>     - CPOL = 0 → SCK idles **LOW**.
>     - CPOL = 1 → SCK idles **HIGH**.
>
> - **CPHA (Clock Phase)** — defines on which SCK edge data is sampled.
>     - CPHA = 0 → sampled on the **leading (first) edge**, shifted on trailing edge.
>     - CPHA = 1 → sampled on the **trailing (second) edge**, shifted on leading edge.
>
> | Mode | CPOL | CPHA | SCK Idle | Sample Edge |
> |---|---|---|---|---|
> | **0** | 0 | 0 | LOW | Rising |
> | **1** | 0 | 1 | LOW | Falling |
> | **2** | 1 | 0 | HIGH | Falling |
> | **3** | 1 | 1 | HIGH | Rising |
>
> **SSD1306 uses Mode 0 (CPOL=0, CPHA=0)** (4 marks for table + 3 marks for justification). Justification: the SSD1306 expects the clock to be low when idle (its internal shift register latches on the first rising edge). Most microcontrollers (8051, STM32, Arduino) default to Mode 0, eliminating the need for reconfiguration. Using any other mode will result in no display activity or corrupted commands.
>
> **Part (b) — SSD1306 Initialization (7 marks):**
>
> | Step | Opcode (Hex) | Description | Marks |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | 1 | `0xAE` | Display OFF (start with display off) | 0.5 |
> | 2 | `0xD5`, `0x80` | Set display clock divide ratio to default | 0.5 |
> | 3 | `0xA8`, `0x3F` | Set multiplex to 64 (for 128×64) | 1 |
> | 4 | `0xD3`, `0x00` | Set display offset to 0 | 0.5 |
> | 5 | `0x40` | Set start line to 0 | 0.5 |
> | 6 | `0x8D`, `0x14` | **Enable charge pump** (essential for 3.3 V OLEDs) | 1.5 |
> | 7 | `0x20`, `0x00` | Set addressing mode to Horizontal | 1 |
> | 8 | `0xA1` | Segment remap (mirror horizontally) | 0.5 |
> | 9 | `0xC8` | COM output scan direction remap | 0.5 |
> | 10 | `0xDA`, `0x12` | COM pins hardware config for 128×64 | 0.5 |
> | 11 | `0x81`, `0xCF` | Set contrast to mid-high | 0.5 |
> | 12 | `0xD9`, `0xF1` | Set pre-charge period | 0.5 |
> | 13 | `0xDB`, `0x40` | Set VCOMH deselect level | 0.5 |
> | 14 | `0xA4` | Display from RAM (not entire ON) | 0.5 |
> | 15 | `0xAF` | **Display ON** | 1 |
>
> **Role of the charge pump command `0x8D 0x14` (1 mark):** The SSD1306 needs an internal high voltage (~7–9 V) to drive the OLED pixels. When the module is powered from 3.3 V, an external high voltage is unavailable. The charge pump command enables the on-chip DC-DC converter, generating this high voltage internally. **Without this command, the OLED remains blank even after `0xAF`.**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the 4.7 kΩ pull-ups on SDA/SCL (–2 marks):** The I²C bus is open-drain. Without pull-ups, the lines stay LOW and the master sees no ACK. This is the #1 reason for "LCD not responding" in the lab.
>
> 2. **Skipping the 15 ms power-on delay (–1 mark):** The HD44780 needs this delay to initialize its internal oscillator. Skipping it causes a partially-initialized LCD that displays only blocks.
>
> 3. **Wrong SPI mode for SSD1306 (–2 marks):** The default in some libraries is Mode 0, but if you manually configure SPI to Mode 3 (e.g., on STM32 HAL), the OLED will silently fail to acknowledge any commands. Always verify with logic analyzer.
>
> 4. **MSB vs LSB confusion (–1 mark):** SSD1306 and HD44780 both expect **MSB-first**. Some MCUs default to LSB-first; explicitly configure MSB-first in your SPI peripheral.
>
> 5. **Not sending the charge pump command for 3.3 V OLEDs (–2 marks):** A black screen on a 3.3 V OLED almost always means the charge pump is disabled.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Revise this list 30 minutes before the exam.**

- **Two protocols:** SPI (4-wire, full-duplex, fast, hardware CS) and I²C (2-wire, half-duplex, addressable, needs pull-ups).
- **I²C mandatory hardware:** **4.7 kΩ pull-up resistors** on SDA and SCL to VCC. Without them, the bus fails.
- **Typical I²C LCD address:** `0x27` (or `0x3F` for the PCF8574A variant).
- **Address byte sent on bus = `(address << 1) \vert R/W`**, e.g., `0x27 << 1 = 0x4E` for write.
- **HD44780 mandatory init sequence:** wait 15 ms → `0x30` → wait 4.1 ms → `0x30` → wait 100 µs → `0x30` → `0x20` → `0x28` → `0x0C` → `0x01` (wait 1.64 ms) → `0x06`.
- **4-bit mode rule:** Every command/data byte must be transmitted as **two nibbles**, high nibble first.
- **PCF8574 pin mapping:** P7–P4 = LCD D7–D4, P3 = Backlight, P2 = Enable, P1 = R/W, P0 = RS.
- **Enable pulse:** Data is latched on the **HIGH-to-LOW transition** of the EN pin. A minimum 1 µs HIGH time is required.
- **DDRAM address formula:** `0x80 + position` (line 1), `0xC0 + position` (line 2).
- **SPI Mode for SSD1306 = Mode 0** (CPOL = 0, CPHA = 0). Sample on rising edge.
- **SPI byte order:** Always **MSB-first** for HD44780 and SSD1306.
- **OLED charge pump:** Command `0x8D 0x14` is **mandatory** for 3.3 V OLED modules; without it, display remains blank.
- **CGRAM for custom characters:** Addresses `0x40`–`0x7F`. Up to 8 custom 5×8 characters can be defined.
- **Three memory regions of HD44780:** DDRAM (display data), CGRAM (custom chars), CGROM (built-in font, contains ASCII).
- **Common observation errors:** Dark blocks on line 1 only → contrast pot mis-adjusted; squares on both lines → init sequence failed; garbled characters → wrong nibble order or wrong PCF8574 address.
- **KTU viva favourite:** *"What happens if you send 0x30 only once during init?"* — Answer: on most modern LCDs the second-line position may not work, and the controller may interpret subsequent commands in 8-bit mode by default.
- **Lab record must include:** Circuit diagram, initialization flowchart, full C code, screenshot/photo of working display, and the I²C address found by the scanner.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Real-Time Clock
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECL408 - MICROCONTROLLERS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Real-Time Clock

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

A **Real-Time Clock (RTC)** is a dedicated hardware peripheral integrated circuit (IC) specifically engineered to maintain accurate tracking of time and date information — encompassing seconds, minutes, hours, day, date, month, and year — even when the host microcontroller is powered down, reset, or executing unrelated computational tasks. In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme Microcontrollers Lab (PCECL408)**, the RTC experiment typically involves interfacing a serial RTC IC such as the **DS1307** (with an external 32.768 kHz quartz crystal) or the **DS3231** (with an internal temperature-compensated crystal) to a target microcontroller through the **Inter-Integrated Circuit (I²C / IIC / TWI)** two-wire serial bus.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly expects students to demonstrate mastery of the I²C (Inter-Integrated Circuit) communication protocol as the primary bus for RTC interfacing. Students must understand both the hardware signal lines (SDA — Serial Data, SCL — Serial Clock) and the software-level address/data framing required to read from and write to RTC internal registers.

The DS1307 and DS3231 store time-keeping information in **Binary-Coded Decimal (BCD)** format within eight dedicated time/date registers, accessible at addresses **0x00 through 0x07**. Each register holds one time field (e.g., 0x00 → Seconds, 0x01 → Minutes, 0x02 → Hours, 0x03 → Day-of-Week, 0x04 → Date, 0x05 → Month, 0x06 → Year). The fixed slave address of the DS1307 is **0x68** (binary `1101000`) on the I²C bus.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of an RTC as a **battery-backed digital wristwatch mounted on your circuit board**.

| Component | Analogy in Real Life | Function in the Circuit |
|---|---|---|
| RTC IC | The watch chip itself | Stores and increments time |
| 32.768 kHz Crystal | The ticking mechanism of a quartz watch | Provides precise 1-second ticks |
| Coin Cell Battery (CR2032) | The watch battery | Keeps time running when main board is OFF |
| I²C Bus (SDA + SCL) | The USB cable to your PC | Lets the microcontroller ask "what time is it?" |

**Why not just use the microcontroller's internal timer?** A novice might assume the microcontroller's built-in timers (e.g., Timer0, Timer1, Timer2 in 8051; or SysTick in ARM Cortex-M) are sufficient. However, internal timers are **volatile** — they lose count on power-off and require re-initialization. An RTC solves this elegantly: it ticks independently using an ultra-low-power oscillator and a backup battery, so even if your main microcontroller is asleep, removed, or rebooted, the RTC never stops counting.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Concept:** The RTC maintains time **independently** of the CPU. The microcontroller's job is simply to **poll** (read) or **set** (write) the time via I²C when needed. This decoupling is the fundamental architectural insight every KTU examiner expects you to articulate.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

- **Crystal Frequency:** **32.768 kHz** ($f_{xtal} = 32768 \text{ Hz}$)
- **Divide Ratio:** $32768 = 2^{15}$ → exactly **15 binary divisions** yield a perfect **1 Hz** (1-second) tick
- **Typical RTC Current Draw:** **~1.5 µA** at 3 V (DS1307), **~110 µA** (DS3231 active, ~840 nA timekeeping)
- **Backup Battery:** **CR2032 (3 V, 220 mAh)** → estimated life **>10 years**
- **I²C Standard Mode Speed:** **100 kHz**; Fast Mode: **400 kHz**
- **Pull-up Resistor Value:** Typically **4.7 kΩ** on both SDA and SCL lines to **Vcc** (5 V or 3.3 V)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — How an RTC Works

The RTC IC operates on a clean separation of concerns across three subsystems:

### 2.1.1 The Oscillator Subsystem
- A **32.768 kHz quartz crystal** is connected between the **X1** and **X2** pins of the RTC.
- Quartz exhibits the **piezoelectric effect**: it vibrates mechanically at a precisely cut resonant frequency when an oscillating electric field is applied.
- The IC contains an **inverting amplifier** with the crystal in its feedback loop, forming a **Pierce oscillator**.
- The output is a stable square wave at exactly **32768 Hz**.

### 2.1.2 The Frequency Divider Subsystem
- A chain of **15 flip-flops** divides the 32.768 kHz signal by $2^{15}$:

$$\frac{32768 \text{ Hz}}{2^{15}} = \frac{32768}{32768} = 1 \text{ Hz}$$

- This produces a perfect **1 pulse per second (1 PPS)** signal used to increment the seconds counter.

### 2.1.3 The Register / Counter Subsystem
- Seven cascaded BCD counters track **seconds → minutes → hours → day-of-week → date → month → year**.
- Each counter rolls over automatically (e.g., 59 seconds → 00 minutes, 23 hours → 00 date, etc.).
- Year register handles **leap-year compensation** automatically through the year 2099.

## 2.2 The I²C Protocol Stack for RTC Communication

The DS1307/DS3231 is a **slave-only** I²C device. Communication always follows a strict frame:

> **START → Slave Address (7-bit: 0x68) + R/W̅ bit → ACK → Register Address → ACK → Data Byte(s) → ACK → STOP**

| Frame Element | Direction | Width | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| START (S) | Master → Bus | 1 bit | Signals beginning of a transaction; SDA falls while SCL is HIGH |
| Slave Address | Master → Bus | 7 bits | Identifies the RTC (0x68 for DS1307) |
| R/W̅ | Master → Bus | 1 bit | 1 = Read, 0 = Write |
| ACK | Slave → Master | 1 bit | Acknowledgment (SDA LOW during SCL HIGH) |
| Register Address | Master → Bus | 8 bits | Pointer to which RTC register to access |
| Data | Both directions | 8 bits | Actual time/date byte |
| STOP (P) | Master → Bus | 1 bit | SDA rises while SCL is HIGH |

## 2.3 BCD Encoding — The Critical Translator

The RTC stores all time values in **Binary-Coded Decimal (BCD)** format, not in plain binary. In BCD, each decimal digit is stored in its own 4-bit nibble.

| Decimal | Binary | BCD |
|---|---|---|
| 25 (seconds) | 0001 1001 | 0010 0101 |
| 59 (minutes) | 0011 1011 | 0101 1001 |
| 12 (hours) | 0000 1100 | 0001 0010 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield for KTU:** If the RTC returns `0x45` (binary `0100 0101`) for the minutes register, the microcontroller **MUST** convert BCD → Decimal: `(0x45 >> 4) × 10 + (0x45 & 0x0F) = 4 × 10 + 5 = 45 minutes`. Failure to perform this conversion is the **#1 source of bugs** in RTC lab sessions.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter / Concept | Formula / Value | Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Oscillator division ratio | $2^{15} = 32768$ | dimensionless | Yields exactly 1 Hz |
| Tick frequency | $f_{tick} = f_{xtal} \div 2^{15}$ | Hz | $32768 \div 32768 = 1$ |
| I²C SCL period | $T_{SCL} = 1 \div f_{SCL}$ | seconds | For 100 kHz → $T_{SCL} = 10\,\mu s$ |
| DS1307 slave address | `1101000` (0x68) | 7-bit | Fixed at the factory |
| BCD → Decimal | $\text{Dec} = (\text{BCD} \gg 4) \times 10 + (\text{BCD} \,\&\, 0x0F)$ | decimal | Standard bit-shift conversion |
| Decimal → BCD | $\text{BCD} = ((\text{Dec} \div 10) \ll 4) \, \vert \, (\text{Dec} \,\%\, 10)$ | BCD byte | Required before writing to RTC |
| Pull-up resistor (typical) | $R_{pullup} = 4.7\,\text{k}\Omega$ | ohms | Connected SDA→Vcc and SCL→Vcc |
| Backup battery | $V_{bat} = 3.0$ | V | CR2032 lithium coin cell |
| Year register range | 00–99 | years | DS1307 needs century roll-over handling beyond 2099 |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

RTCs are the silent backbone of nearly every embedded product that needs to "remember when":

- **Industrial Data Loggers** — timestamping sensor readings
- **Digital Wall Clocks & Alarm Systems** — timekeeping display
- **Attendance / Access Control Systems** — recording entry/exact times
- **GPS Modules** — providing initial time fix before satellite lock
- **Medical Devices** — patient monitoring, drug-dosing schedules
- **IoT Edge Nodes** — synchronized telemetry with cloud time-series databases

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why 32.768 kHz?

The choice of **32,768 Hz** is not arbitrary — it is engineered for binary divisibility. Let us prove why:

$$
f_{xtal} = 32768 \text{ Hz} = 2^{15} \text{ Hz}
$$

We need a 1 Hz output for 1-second ticks. Therefore the divider chain must have $n$ flip-flops such that:

$$
2^n = \frac{f_{xtal}}{f_{out}} = \frac{2^{15}}{1}
$$

$$
n = 15
$$

Thus a **15-stage binary ripple counter** perfectly produces 1 Hz from a 32.768 kHz crystal.

**Engineering Implication:** $2^n$ divisions always produce an integer Hz, eliminating any fractional error. This is why watch manufacturers worldwide standardized on **32.768 kHz** — it is the highest frequency below ~100 kHz that is a clean power-of-two multiple of 1 Hz. The resulting current consumption is also minimal, making it ideal for battery-powered watches and RTCs.

## 3.2 Derivation: BCD ↔ Decimal Conversion (Mathematical Form)

The RTC operates exclusively on **BCD**, but humans read **decimal**. The conversion is a bijective mapping between the integers $0$ to $99$ and their BCD representations.

Let a BCD byte be $B = b_7 b_6 b_5 b_4 b_3 b_2 b_1 b_0$ (8 bits). The high nibble $H = b_7 b_6 b_5 b_4$ holds the **tens digit**; the low nibble $L = b_3 b_2 b_1 b_0$ holds the **units digit**.

### BCD → Decimal:

$$
D = H \times 10 + L
$$

$$
D = \left( \left\lfloor \frac{B}{16} \right\rfloor \right) \times 10 + (B \bmod 16)
$$

$$
D = (B \gg 4) \times 10 + (B \,\&\, 0x0F)
$$

**Worked Example 1:** Read minutes register, $B = 0x59 = 0101\,1001_2$.

- High nibble $H = 0101_2 = 5$ (tens of minutes)
- Low nibble $L = 1001_2 = 9$ (units of minutes)
- $D = 5 \times 10 + 9 = 59$ minutes ✓

**Worked Example 2:** Read hours register, $B = 0x23 = 0010\,0011_2$.

- $H = 0010_2 = 2$
- $L = 0011_2 = 3$
- $D = 2 \times 10 + 3 = 23$ hours ✓

### Decimal → BCD (used when writing to RTC):

$$
B = (D \div 10) \times 16 + (D \bmod 10)
$$

$$
B = (D \div 10) \ll 4 \, \vert \, (D \bmod 10)
$$

**Worked Example 3:** Set minutes to 47.

- $47 \div 10 = 4$ (tens), $47 \bmod 10 = 7$ (units)
- $B = (4 \ll 4) \, \vert \, 7 = 0x40 \, \vert \, 0x07 = 0x47$
- Writing $0x47$ to register $0x01$ sets minutes to **47** ✓

## 3.3 Derivation: I²C Bit-Banging Timing (Software I²C)

In many microcontroller labs (especially with 8051/Arduino), students manually toggle GPIO pins to generate I²C signals. For a target SCL frequency of $f_{SCL} = 100$ kHz:

$$
T_{SCL} = \frac{1}{f_{SCL}} = \frac{1}{100 \times 10^3} = 10 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 10\,\mu s
$$

$$
T_{half\_SCL} = \frac{T_{SCL}}{2} = 5\,\mu s
$$

In bit-banging with an $f_{CPU} = 12$ MHz clock, one machine cycle is $1\,\mu s$. Therefore the delay loop must busy-wait approximately **5 NOPs** in each half-period.

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical insight:** When writing the I²C delay routine, students often forget that the SDA line must be stable while SCL is HIGH. The data line can only change **when SCL is LOW**. SDA transitions while SCL is HIGH define the **START** (falling edge) and **STOP** (rising edge) conditions — confusing these is a classic KTU viva trap question.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: RTC Register Map (DS1307)

| Addr | Register | Bit 7 | Bit 6 | Bit 5 | Bit 4 | Bit 3 | Bit 2 | Bit 1 | Bit 0 | Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0x00 | Seconds | CH | 10 sec | 8 sec | 4 sec | 2 sec | 1 sec | 0.5 s? | 0.25 s? | 00–59 |
| 0x01 | Minutes | 0 | 10 min | 8 min | 4 min | 2 min | 1 min | — | — | 00–59 |
| 0x02 | Hours | 12/24 | 0 | 10 hr (AM/PM, 10hr24) | 4 hr | 2 hr | 1 hr | — | — | 00–23 |
| 0x03 | Day | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | Day | Day | Day | 1–7 |
| 0x04 | Date | 0 | 0 | 10 d | 4 d | 2 d | 1 d | — | — | 01–31 |
| 0x05 | Month | 0 | 0 | 0 | 10 m | 8 m | 4 m | 2 m | 1 m | 01–12 |
| 0x06 | Year | 10 y | 8 y | 4 y | 2 y | 1 y | — | — | — | 00–99 |
| 0x07 | Control | OUT | 0 | 0 | SQWE | 0 | 0 | RS1 | RS0 | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CH bit (Bit 7 of 0x00):** The **Clock Halt** bit. When set to `1`, the oscillator is disabled and the RTC stops counting. After power-up with a fresh battery, **CH is typically `1`** — you must clear it (`0`) by writing `0x00` to register 0x00 to start the clock. Forgetting this step is one of the most common RTC lab failures.

## 3.5 Derivation: Reading Multiple Bytes in a Burst (Auto-Increment)

To read the full time efficiently, the master writes the starting address `0x00`, generates a **repeated START** (Sr), then issues a read command. The RTC's internal **register pointer auto-increments** after every ACK, allowing a sequential burst read:

**Read sequence:** S → 0xD0 → A → 0x00 → A → Sr → 0xD1 → A → [Sec] A [Min] A [Hr] A ... [Yr] N̄ → P

Where:
- `0xD0` = slave write address (`1101000` + R/W̅=0)
- `0xD1` = slave read address (`1101000` + R/W̅=1)
- `A` = ACK from slave
- `N̄` = NOT-ACK from master (signals "last byte")
- `P` = STOP

This yields 7 consecutive bytes (0x00 → 0x06) containing seconds through year in a single transaction — far more efficient than seven separate read cycles.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 System-Level Block Diagram (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph MCU["MICROCONTROLLER UNIT 8051 / ARM / Arduino"]
        CPU["CPU Core"]
        GPIOs["GPIO Pins"]
        I2C_M["I2C Master Controller"]
        DISP["LCD Display Driver"]
        CPU --> GPIOs
        CPU --> I2C_M
        CPU --> DISP
    end

    subgraph BUS["I2C BUS"]
        SDA_LINE["SDA Line - Serial Data"]
        SCL_LINE["SCL Line - Serial Clock"]
    end

    subgraph RTC["DS1307 RTC MODULE"]
        XTAL["32.768 kHz Quartz Crystal"]
        AMP["Internal Pierce Oscillator"]
        DIV["15-Stage Binary Divider"]
        BCD_CNT["BCD Counter Chain - Sec Min Hr Date Mon Yr"]
        REG_BANK["Register Bank 0x00 to 0x07"]
        BAT["CR2032 Backup Battery 3V"]
        XTAL --> AMP
        AMP --> DIV
        DIV --> BCD_CNT
        BCD_CNT --> REG_BANK
        BAT -.->|VBAT| RTC
    end

    subgraph LCD["16x2 LCD DISPLAY"]
        LCD_SCR["LCD Screen showing HH:MM:SS"]
    end

    GPIOs --> SDA_LINE
    GPIOs --> SCL_LINE
    SDA_LINE --> REG_BANK
    SCL_LINE --> REG_BANK
    I2C_M -.->|read time| REG_BANK
    REG_BANK -.->|BCD data burst| I2C_M
    I2C_M --> DISP
    DISP --> LCD_SCR
```

## 4.2 I²C Transaction Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant M as Master (Microcontroller)
    participant R as RTC Slave (DS1307 addr 0x68)
    participant L as LCD Display

    Note over M,R: Step 1: Write initial time to RTC
    M->>R: START condition (SDA falls, SCL high)
    M->>R: Slave Address 0xD0 (write)
    R-->>M: ACK
    M->>R: Register Address 0x00 (seconds)
    R-->>M: ACK
    M->>R: Seconds value (e.g., 0x00)
    R-->>M: ACK
    M->>R: Minutes value (e.g., 0x30)
    R-->>M: ACK
    M->>R: Hours value (e.g., 0x14)
    R-->>M: ACK
    M->>R: STOP condition (SDA rises, SCL high)

    Note over M,R: Step 2: Periodic polling every 1 second
    loop Every 1 second
        M->>R: START
        M->>R: 0xD0 (write)
        R-->>M: ACK
        M->>R: 0x00 (pointer to seconds)
        R-->>M: ACK
        M->>R: Repeated START (Sr)
        M->>R: 0xD1 (read)
        R-->>M: ACK
        R-->>M: Seconds BCD byte
        M->>R: ACK
        R-->>M: Minutes BCD byte
        M->>R: ACK
        R-->>M: Hours BCD byte
        M-->>R: NACK (last byte)
        M->>R: STOP

        Note over M,L: Microcontroller converts BCD to decimal
        M->>L: Display formatted time string HH:MM:SS
    end
```

## 4.3 Internal Architecture of the DS1307

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DS1307["DS1307 INTERNAL ARCHITECTURE"]
        P1["Pin X1"]:::pin
        P2["Pin X2"]:::pin
        XTAL_INT["32.768 kHz Quartz"]
        P1 --- XTAL_INT
        XTAL_INT --- P2
        XTAL_INT --> OSC["Oscillator Circuit"]
        OSC --> DIV15["Divide by 2^15 Counter"]
        DIV15 --> SEC_CNT["Seconds Counter BCD"]
        SEC_CNT --> MIN_CNT["Minutes Counter BCD"]
        MIN_CNT --> HR_CNT["Hours Counter BCD"]
        HR_CNT --> DAY_CNT["Day Counter"]
        DAY_CNT --> DATE_CNT["Date Counter BCD"]
        DATE_CNT --> MON_CNT["Month Counter BCD"]
        MON_CNT --> YR_CNT["Year Counter BCD"]
        YR_CNT --> SRAM["56-byte Battery-Backed SRAM 0x08 to 0x3F"]
        YR_CNT --> REGMAP["Register File 0x00 to 0x07"]
        REGMAP --> I2C_ENG["I2C Interface Engine"]
        I2C_ENG --> PIN_SDA["Pin SDA"]
        I2C_ENG --> PIN_SCL["Pin SCL"]
    end

    classDef pin fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.4 Software Flow: RTC Reading Routine

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initialize I2C bus: set SDA and SCL as outputs, set HIGH] --> B[Initialize LCD: 8-bit mode, clear display, cursor off]
    B --> C[Optionally set initial time: write 0x00 to 0x07 registers]
    C --> D[Start main loop]
    D --> E[Generate START condition on I2C]
    E --> F[Send slave address 0xD0 + write]
    F --> G{ACK received?}
    G -->|No| ERR[Error: RTC not responding - check wiring]
    G -->|Yes| H[Send register pointer 0x00]
    H --> I[Generate Repeated START]
    I --> J[Send slave address 0xD1 + read]
    J --> K[Read 7 bytes: Sec Min Hr Day Date Mon Yr with ACK between bytes and NACK on last]
    K --> L[Generate STOP condition]
    L --> M[Convert each BCD byte to decimal using shift-mask formula]
    M --> N[Format string: HH:MM:SS and DD/MM/YYYY]
    N --> O[Send formatted string to LCD]
    O --> P[Delay approximately 1000 ms using software delay]
    P --> D
    ERR --> Q[Halt program or blink error LED]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define a Real-Time Clock (RTC) and explain why it is preferred over microcontroller internal timers for timekeeping. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
A **Real-Time Clock (RTC)** is a dedicated low-power IC that maintains accurate tracking of time (seconds, minutes, hours, date, month, year) using an external 32.768 kHz quartz crystal and a backup battery. It operates independently of the host microcontroller.

**Why preferred over internal timers:**
1. **Non-volatility:** RTC retains time during power-off via backup battery; internal timers reset to zero on power-down.
2. **Accuracy:** Quartz crystal offers ppm-level accuracy; internal RC oscillators drift with temperature.
3. **CPU offloading:** The RTC increments time autonomously, freeing the CPU from periodic interrupt-based counting.
4. **Long-term reliability:** RTCs include automatic leap-year compensation and month-length handling (28/29/30/31 days).

---

### Question 2: Explain the role of the 32.768 kHz crystal in an RTC. Why is this specific frequency used? `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
The **32.768 kHz quartz crystal** acts as the frequency reference for the RTC's oscillator circuit. The IC contains a **Pierce oscillator** that drives the crystal, producing a stable square wave at exactly 32,768 Hz.

**Why this specific frequency?**
- $32{,}768 = 2^{15}$
- A 15-stage binary ripple counter divides this frequency down to:

$$\frac{2^{15} \text{ Hz}}{2^{15}} = 1 \text{ Hz}$$

- This produces a perfect **1 pulse-per-second** signal used to increment the seconds counter.
- The choice yields integer divisions (no fractional error), low current consumption (~1 µA), and is the worldwide standard for low-frequency timekeeping ICs and digital watches.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A: Design and explain an RTC interfacing system with an 8051 microcontroller using the DS1307. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### Part (a) — Hardware Interfacing and I²C Bus Implementation (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Hardware Connections:**

| 8051 Pin | DS1307 Pin | Function |
|---|---|---|
| P1.0 | SDA | Serial Data (open-drain, needs 4.7 kΩ pull-up to Vcc) |
| P1.1 | SCL | Serial Clock (open-drain, needs 4.7 kΩ pull-up to Vcc) |
| — | X1, X2 | Connect 32.768 kHz crystal with 12 pF load capacitors to GND |
| — | Vbat | Connect CR2032 (+3 V) coin cell |
| GND | GND | Common ground reference |
| +5 V | Vcc | Main power supply |

**I²C Bit-Banging Implementation Logic:**

Because many 8051 variants lack a dedicated hardware I²C peripheral, the protocol is implemented in software using GPIO bit-banging. The key subroutines required are:

1. **`I2C_Start()`** — Generate START condition: set SDA HIGH → SCL HIGH → SDA LOW → SCL LOW.
2. **`I2C_Stop()`** — Generate STOP condition: set SDA LOW → SCL HIGH → SDA HIGH.
3. **`I2C_Write(byte)`** — Transmit 8 bits MSB first, toggle SCL for each bit, check ACK after the 8th clock.
4. **`I2C_Read(ack)`** — Receive 8 bits MSB first, send ACK or NACK at the end.
5. **`delay_us(t)`** — Software delay tuned to achieve ~100 kHz SCL frequency.

**DS1307 Slave Address:** `1101000` (0x68) for the 7-bit address. With R/W̅ bit appended:
- Write mode → transmit `0xD0`
- Read mode → transmit `0xD1`

#### Part (b) — Reading Time from the RTC and Display on LCD (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Burst Read Sequence**

To read current time efficiently, a burst read of 7 bytes from address 0x00 is performed:

```
I2C_Start()
I2C_Write(0xD0)        // Slave address + write
I2C_Write(0x00)        // Pointer to seconds register
I2C_Start()            // Repeated START
I2C_Write(0xD1)        // Slave address + read
sec = I2C_Read(ACK)    // Read seconds with ACK
min = I2C_Read(ACK)    // Read minutes with ACK
hr  = I2C_Read(ACK)    // Read hours with ACK
day = I2C_Read(ACK)    // Read day-of-week
date= I2C_Read(ACK)    // Read date
mon = I2C_Read(ACK)    // Read month
year= I2C_Read(NACK)   // Read year, NACK signals last byte
I2C_Stop()
```

**Step 2: BCD-to-Decimal Conversion**

Each byte is converted using bit operations:

```
sec_dec = ((sec >> 4) & 0x0F) * 10 + (sec & 0x0F);
min_dec = ((min >> 4) & 0x0F) * 10 + (min & 0x0F);
hr_dec  = ((hr  >> 4) & 0x0F) * 10 + (hr  & 0x0F);
```

**Step 3: LCD Display Formatting**

Convert each decimal value to ASCII by adding `0x30` and place in a character array:

```
buffer[0] = (hr_dec / 10) + '0';
buffer[1] = (hr_dec % 10) + '0';
buffer[2] = ':';
buffer[3] = (min_dec / 10) + '0';
buffer[4] = (min_dec % 10) + '0';
buffer[5] = ':';
buffer[6] = (sec_dec / 10) + '0';
buffer[7] = (sec_dec % 10) + '0';
buffer[8] = '\0';
```

Send `buffer` to the LCD using `LCD_String()`.

**Step 4: Periodic Update**

Insert a software delay loop of approximately 1000 ms between successive reads, then loop back to Step 1 to continuously refresh the display.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Where students lose marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to clear the CH bit** (Bit 7 of register 0x00) on first initialization — the clock will not start.
> 2. **Mixing up slave addresses** — 0xD0 is for write, 0xD1 is for read; examiners specifically check this.
> 3. **Skipping the BCD conversion** — displaying raw BCD values (e.g., showing `0x45` instead of `45`) is an automatic 2-mark deduction.
> 4. **Missing pull-up resistors** on SDA/SCL — without them, the lines will float and I²C will fail silently. Always mention the 4.7 kΩ pull-up in the circuit diagram.

---

### Question B: With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of the DS1307 RTC and describe the function of its internal registers. Write the I²C protocol steps to set the RTC time to 14:35:20 (24-hour format). `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### Part (a) — DS1307 Architecture and Register Description (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram (textual representation):**

```
                +5V Vcc
                  |
                [DS1307 IC]
                  |
   X1 ---[32.768 kHz Crystal]--- X2
                  |
        [Oscillator + Pierce Amp]
                  |
        [÷ 2^15 Binary Divider]  → 1 Hz tick
                  |
        [BCD Counter Chain: Sec → Min → Hr → Day → Date → Mon → Yr]
                  |
        [Register File (0x00–0x07)] ↔ [56-byte SRAM (0x08–0x3F)]
                  |
        [I²C Interface Engine] ↔ SDA, SCL pins
                  |
              Vbat ← CR2032 (3V)
```

**Register Functions:**

| Address | Name | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 0x00 | Seconds | BCD 00–59, Bit 7 = CH (Clock Halt) |
| 0x01 | Minutes | BCD 00–59 |
| 0x02 | Hours | BCD 00–23 (24-hour mode) or 01–12 (12-hour mode); Bit 6 = 12/24 select |
| 0x03 | Day | Day-of-week, 1–7 (user-defined) |
| 0x04 | Date | BCD 01–31 (auto-rolled per month length) |
| 0x05 | Month | BCD 01–12 |
| 0x06 | Year | BCD 00–99 |
| 0x07 | Control | Bit 4 = SQWE (square-wave enable); Bits 0–1 = RS1/RS0 (output frequency select) |

#### Part (b) — I²C Protocol Steps to Set Time to 14:35:20 (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Convert decimal values to BCD**

- 20 seconds → $(20/10) \ll 4 \, \vert \, (20\%10) = 0x20$
- 35 minutes → $(35/10) \ll 4 \, \vert \, (35\%10) = 0x35$
- 14 hours (24-hour mode) → $(14/10) \ll 4 \, \vert \, (14\%10) = 0x14$

**Step 2: I²C Write Frame**

```
I2C_Start()
I2C_Write(0xD0)        // Slave address + write bit (0x68 << 1 | 0)
I2C_Write(0x00)        // Set register pointer to seconds (0x00)
I2C_Write(0x20)        // Write seconds = 20 (BCD)
I2C_Write(0x35)        // Write minutes = 35 (BCD), pointer auto-increments
I2C_Write(0x14)        // Write hours = 14 (BCD), 24-hour mode (Bit 6 = 0)
I2C_Stop()
```

**Step 3: Verification**

After ~100 ms, perform a burst read from address 0x00 to confirm the stored values. The RTC will internally toggle its seconds register every 1 second automatically — no further write is needed for counting.

**Critical Configuration Note:** The **CH bit** (Bit 7 of the seconds register) must be `0` to enable the oscillator. Since 0x20 has Bit 7 = 0, the clock is enabled automatically. If writing `0x80` or higher, the oscillator would halt.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU Exams:**

- **RTC = battery-backed, crystal-driven timekeeper IC** that runs independently of the CPU.
- **Slave address of DS1307 = 0x68 (7-bit)** → write byte `0xD0`, read byte `0xD1`.
- **Crystal frequency 32.768 kHz** because $32{,}768 = 2^{15}$ divides cleanly to **1 Hz** (1-second tick).
- **Time stored in BCD**, not binary — always convert using $(B \gg 4) \times 10 + (B \,\&\, 0x0F)$ on read; $(D/10) \ll 4 \, \vert \, (D \bmod 10)$ on write.
- **I²C requires 2 lines (SDA, SCL)** with **4.7 kΩ pull-up resistors** to Vcc — open-drain configuration.
- **CH bit (Bit 7 of 0x00)** must be cleared on first use, otherwise the oscillator stays disabled.
- **7 registers (0x00–0x06)** hold time; **register 0x07** is the control register (square-wave settings).
- **Burst read with repeated START** is the efficient way to fetch all 7 time bytes in one transaction.
- **56 bytes of battery-backed SRAM** (addresses 0x08–0x3F) are available for general-purpose non-volatile storage.
- **DS3231** is a higher-accuracy alternative with **internal TCXO** (±2 ppm) — no external crystal needed.
- **Backup battery (CR2032, 3 V)** lasts **>10 years** due to RTC's ~1.5 µA quiescent current.
- **I²C standard mode** = 100 kHz; **fast mode** = 400 kHz. Use appropriate software delays for bit-banging.
- **START condition:** SDA falls while SCL is HIGH. **STOP condition:** SDA rises while SCL is HIGH. Confusing these is a common viva trap.
- **ACK = SDA LOW** during the 9th clock pulse; **NACK = SDA HIGH** (signals last byte read).
- **Year 2000 bug note:** DS1307's year register rolls over from 99 to 00 — beyond 2099, century handling must be done in software.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Develop 8051 Microcontroller Programs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECL408 - MICROCONTROLLERS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Develop 8051 Microcontroller Programs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**8051 Microcontroller Programming** is the process of writing, assembling/compiling, debugging, and executing instruction sequences (in 8051 Assembly Language or Embedded C) that control the internal registers, memory-mapped Special Function Registers (SFRs), and I/O ports of the **Intel MCS-51 family microcontroller** to accomplish a defined hardware task.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PCECL408 – Microcontrollers Lab**, this experiment covers the development of programs using:

1. **8051 Assembly Language (ALP)** — mnemonic-level programming using the official MCS-51 instruction set.
2. **Embedded C for 8051** — using the **Keil µVision IDE** with the **C51 compiler** to generate `.HEX` files for target hardware (typically the **AT89C51 / AT89S52** chip from Atmel/Microchip).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**
> A typical 8051 program must explicitly handle three layers:
> - **Hardware Abstraction Layer** → SFR configuration (P0, P1, P2, P3, TCON, TMOD, SCON, IE, etc.)
> - **Algorithmic Layer** → loop, branch, arithmetic, logic
> - **Timing/Sequencing Layer** → delay subroutines, interrupt servicing

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Restaurant Kitchen"

Imagine the 8051 microcontroller as a **head chef in a small restaurant kitchen**:

| Kitchen Element | 8051 Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Head Chef's brain (CPU) | **ALU + Accumulator (A)** — does all arithmetic thinking |
| Recipe book on the counter | **Program Memory (ROM, 4 KB)** — stores your `.HEX` code |
| Fridge & pantry shelves | **Internal RAM (128 bytes, 00H–7FH)** — fast scratchpad storage |
| Locked drawers for special tools | **SFR area (80H–FFH)** — pre-defined "drawers" for ports, timers, serial |
| Waiter carrying order tickets | **I/O Ports (P0–P3)** — communicates with the outside world |
| The timer on the wall | **Timers T0 & T1** with their control registers |
| Step-by-step instructions in the recipe | **Mnemonics** like `MOV`, `ADD`, `DJNZ`, `SJMP` |

When you "write an 8051 program," you are essentially **writing a precise recipe** that the chef will execute **one instruction at a time**, fetching it from ROM, decoding it, and acting on the kitchen (peripherals).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 8051 Still Matters in 2024:**
> The 8051 IP core is one of the most **licensed processor architectures in the world** — found inside hundreds of modern SoCs from companies like Silicon Labs, Nuvoton, and Texas Instruments. Mastering it gives a foundational grasp of **register-level embedded programming**.

## 1.3 Two Main Programming Paradigms for 8051

- **Assembly Language Programming (ALP):** Gives 100% hardware control, smallest possible `.HEX` file, and is **mandatory** in KTU viva questions about instruction formats.
- **Embedded C Programming:** Faster development, easier portability across 8051 variants, and the **industry standard** for real-world firmware.

Both paradigms will be addressed in this module experiment.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of an 8051 Assembly Program

Every KTU lab-viva-approved 8051 assembly program follows this **strict skeleton**:

```asm
        ORG     0000H        ; Origin directive — tells assembler to place next code at ROM address 0000H
        LJMP    MAIN         ; Long Jump to skip over interrupt vector space (0003H–002AH)

        ORG     0030H        ; Place actual program after the interrupt vector area
MAIN:   MOV     SP, #60H     ; Re-initialize Stack Pointer to safe upper RAM region
        ; --- your logic starts here ---
        MOV     A, #25H      ; Load immediate value 25H into Accumulator
        ADD     A, #34H      ; A = A + 34H
        MOV     P1, A        ; Send result to Port 1
HERE:   SJMP    HERE         ; Infinite loop — trap the CPU here forever
        END                  ; End of source file directive
```

### Why Each Line Exists (The "Why" Behind the Code)

| Directive / Mnemonic | Function | KTU Viva Significance |
|---|---|---|
| `ORG 0000H` | Sets the assembler's location counter | Confirms awareness of reset vector location |
| `LJMP MAIN` | Skips interrupt vector table (size = 8 bytes each) | Tests understanding of memory map |
| `ORG 0030H` | Reserves safe code area beyond vector table | Demonstrates professional coding style |
| `MOV SP, #60H` | Prevents stack collision with user RAM (30H–7F) | Critical for any subroutine/interrupt |
| `END` | Marks logical end of assembly source | Required for successful assembly |

## 2.2 The Five Addressing Modes of 8051 (High-Yield for KTU)

The 8051 supports **5 (technically 6, depending on classification)** addressing modes. These are **guaranteed viva questions**.

1. **Immediate Addressing** — Operand is a constant → `MOV A, #0FH`
2. **Register Addressing** — Operand is one of R0–R7 → `MOV A, R0`
3. **Direct Addressing** — Operand is an 8-bit RAM/SFR address → `MOV A, 30H` (RAM) or `MOV A, P1` (SFR)
4. **Register Indirect Addressing** — Address is *inside* a register (use only R0 or R1) → `MOV A, @R0`
5. **Indexed Addressing** — Used for lookup tables in ROM → `MOVC A, @A+DPTR`

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic Trick for Exams:**
> **I-R-D-R-I** → **I**mmediate, **R**egister, **D**irect, **R**eg-Indirect, **I**ndexed

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — 8051 Delay Calculation

The delay subroutine is the **single most repeated experiment** in KTU microcontroller labs. You must memorize this formula:

$$T_{delay} = \left[ (C \times T_{machine}) + \text{overhead} \right]$$

Where:

- $T_{machine} = \dfrac{12}{f_{osc}}$ for the standard 8051 (12-clock cycle per machine cycle)
- $C$ = total number of machine cycles consumed by the loop body and final adjustment

### Generic Inner-Loop Formula (Most Common KTU Pattern)

For a nested loop of the form:

```asm
DELAY:  MOV R1, #FFH      ; 1 MC
LOOP1:  MOV R0, #FFH      ; 1 MC
LOOP2:  DJNZ R0, LOOP2    ; 2 MC per iteration × 255
        DJNZ R1, LOOP1    ; 2 MC × 255
        RET               ; 2 MC
```

The total machine cycles are:

$$T_{MC} = 1 + \left[ 1 + (2 \times 255) \right] \times 255 + 2 + \text{LCALL overhead}$$

$$T_{MC} = 1 + (1 + 510) \times 255 + 2 + 2$$

$$T_{MC} = 1 + 511 \times 255 + 4 = 130{,}240 \text{ MC}$$

For a **12 MHz crystal** (standard KTU trainer kits):

$$T_{machine} = \frac{12}{12 \times 10^{6}} = 1 \,\mu s$$

$$\therefore T_{delay} = 130{,}240 \,\mu s \approx 130 \,ms$$

### Quick Reference Table — Crystal vs Machine Cycle

| Crystal Frequency $f_{osc}$ | Machine Cycle $T_{MC}$ | Typical Delay Range Achievable |
|---|---|---|
| **11.0592 MHz** | **1.085 µs** | 1 µs – 500 ms (also gives exact 9600 baud) |
| **12.000 MHz** | **1.000 µs** | Clean math, KTU default |
| **24.000 MHz** | **0.500 µs** | Faster, used in modern AT89 variants |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:**
> In real-time industrial applications (e.g., traffic-light controllers, washing machine timers, RFID readers), the precision of this delay directly determines **system reliability**. A wrong delay formula can cause sensor polling to drift and data corruption.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Multi-Byte Hex Addition (Module-Standard KTU Program)

**Problem Statement:**
Add two 16-bit numbers stored in internal RAM:
- Number 1 (low byte at 30H, high byte at 31H) → 0x3A6F
- Number 2 (low byte at 40H, high byte at 41H) → 0x1B5C
- Store the 16-bit result (low at 50H, high at 51H). Also output the high byte to **P1**.

### Step 1: Algorithm (Plain English)

1. Load low bytes, add with carry cleared, store low result.
2. Load high bytes, **add with carry (ADDC)** to include carry from low-byte addition.
3. Store high result.
4. Output high result to P1.

### Step 2: Assembly Code with Cycle Annotation

```asm
        ORG     0000H
        LJMP    MAIN
        ORG     0030H
MAIN:   MOV     SP, #60H
        CLR     C            ; Clear Carry flag (CRITICAL — AC line 14)
        MOV     A, 30H       ; A = low byte of Number 1
        ADD     A, 40H       ; A = A + low byte of Number 2 (no carry included yet)
        MOV     50H, A       ; Store result low byte
        MOV     A, 31H       ; A = high byte of Number 1
        ADDC    A, 41H       ; A = A + high byte of Number 2 + C-flag
        MOV     51H, A       ; Store result high byte
        MOV     P1, A        ; Display high byte on Port 1 LEDs
STOP:   SJMP    STOP
        END
```

### Step 3: Manual Verification (Step-by-Step Hex Arithmetic)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Number 1} &= 0x3A6F \\
\text{Number 2} &= 0x1B5C \\
\text{Sum} &= 0x3A6F + 0x1B5C
\end{aligned}
$$

**Low-byte addition** (0x6F + 0x5C):

$$
\begin{aligned}
0x6F + 0x5C &= 0xCB \\
\text{Carry out} &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

So `50H = 0xCB`, `C-flag = 0`.

**High-byte addition** (0x3A + 0x1B + 0):

$$
\begin{aligned}
0x3A + 0x1B &= 0x55 \\
\text{Carry out} &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

So `51H = 0x55`, and `P1 = 0x55`.

**Final result →** `0x555C`

### Step 4: Analytical Matrix — Why Each Instruction?

| Step | Instruction | Purpose | Common Student Mistake |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `CLR C` | Ensures low-byte add does not include garbage from previous operation | Forgetting `CLR C` causes incorrect results in random runs |
| 2 | `ADD A, 40H` | Adds low bytes WITHOUT carry | Using `ADDC` here is a **fatal logical error** |
| 3 | `MOV 50H, A` | Persists result for later inspection | Storing in wrong RAM location is hard to debug |
| 4 | `ADDC A, 41H` | Propagates carry correctly | Missing this is the **#1 reason** programs fail in viva |
| 5 | `MOV P1, A` | Provides visual output on KTU trainer LEDs | Students sometimes use wrong port — check hardware map |

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Embedded C Version (Keil µVision Style)

The same program in **Embedded C**:

```c
#include <reg51.h>

void main(void)
{
    unsigned char num1_low  = 0x6F;
    unsigned char num1_high = 0x3A;
    unsigned char num2_low  = 0x5C;
    unsigned char num2_high = 0x1B;
    unsigned char result_low, result_high;
    unsigned int  carry = 0;

    result_low  = num1_low + num2_low;
    if (result_low < num1_low)       /* detect 8-bit overflow */
        carry = 1;
    result_high = num1_high + num2_high + carry;

    P1 = result_high;                /* display high byte on Port 1 */
    while(1);                        /* trap CPU */
}
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Differences:** In Keil C51, the `sfr` keyword automatically maps the symbolic names (e.g., `P1`) to their SFR addresses (0x90). The compiler then generates the equivalent assembly shown in Section 3.1.

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Generating an Exact 50 ms Delay (Derivation)

**Task:** Generate a 50 ms delay using an **11.0592 MHz crystal**.

### Step 1: Compute Machine Cycle

$$
T_{MC} = \frac{12}{f_{osc}} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^{6}} \approx 1.085 \,\mu s
$$

### Step 2: Required Machine Cycles

$$
N = \frac{50 \times 10^{-3}}{1.085 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 46{,}083 \text{ MC}
$$

### Step 3: Single Loop using R0 with DJNZ

```asm
DELAY:  MOV R0, #COUNT       ; 1 MC
LOOP:   DJNZ R0, LOOP        ; 2 MC per iteration
        RET                  ; 2 MC
```

Total MC = $1 + (2 \times \text{COUNT}) + 2$

Setting this equal to N:

$$
\begin{aligned}
1 + 2 \cdot \text{COUNT} + 2 &= 46{,}083 \\
2 \cdot \text{COUNT} &= 46{,}080 \\
\text{COUNT} &= 23{,}040 = 0x5A00
\end{aligned}
$$

Since 0x5A00 > 0xFF, we must use a **nested loop** (R1 outer, R0 inner).

### Step 4: Nested Loop Selection

Choose inner count `R0 = 230` (0xE6) and solve for outer count `R1`:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= 1 + [1 + (2 \times 230)] \times R1 + 2 + \text{LCALL\_overhead} \\
46{,}083 &= 1 + 461 \times R1 + 2 + 2 \\
R1 &\approx 99.95 \rightarrow \text{use } R1 = 100
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Delay Subroutine:**

```asm
DELAY:  MOV R1, #100        ; outer loop count (1 MC)
OUTER:  MOV R0, #230        ; inner loop count (1 MC)
INNER:  DJNZ R0, INNER      ; 2 MC × 230 = 460 MC
        DJNZ R1, OUTER      ; 2 MC × 100 = 200 MC
        RET                 ; 2 MC
```

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 8051 Program Development Workflow

The following **Mermaid block diagram** depicts the complete toolchain flow when developing a program for the 8051 microcontroller, as required in the KTU lab curriculum.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Source Code Editor] --> B{Choice of Language}
    B --> C[ASM Source File .asm]
    B --> D[C Source File .c]
    C --> E[Assembler A51]
    D --> F[Keil C51 Compiler]
    E --> G[Object File .OBJ]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Linker LX51]
    H --> I[Absolute Object .OMF]
    I --> J[OH51 Object-to-HEX Converter]
    J --> K[Intel HEX File .HEX]
    K --> L[Flash Programming Software]
    L --> M[8051 Target Board / Trainer Kit]
    M --> N[In-Circuit Debugging with Keil Monitor]
    N --> O[Final Verified Output on LEDs / LCD / Motor]
```

### Node-by-Node Explanation

- **A → B:** The student writes source code in either assembly (`.asm`) or C (`.c`).
- **E / F:** The assembler (A51) or compiler (C51) translates source into object code.
- **H:** The linker LX51 resolves all symbol references and assigns absolute ROM/RAM addresses.
- **J:** The OH51 utility converts the absolute object into **Intel HEX format** — the universal format accepted by every 8051 programmer.
- **L:** Programming tools like **FlashMagic**, **progISP**, or the Keil built-in flash loader write the HEX into the AT89C51/S52 chip's flash ROM.
- **M → N:** The KTU trainer kit (or Proteus simulation) executes the program; the student verifies via LEDs, LCD, or a logic probe.

## 4.2 8051 Internal Memory Architecture (Block-Level View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ROM[Program Memory 4 KB]
        R1[0000H - Reset Vector]
        R2[0003H - Ext Int 0]
        R3[000BH - Timer 0]
        R4[0013H - Ext Int 1]
        R5[001BH - Timer 1]
        R6[0023H - Serial Port]
        R7[002BH - Reserved]
        R8[0030H onwards - USER CODE]
    end
    subgraph RAM[Data Memory 128 Bytes]
        D1[00H-1FH - Register Banks RB0-RB3]
        D2[20H-2FH - Bit Addressable Area]
        D3[30H-7FH - General Purpose RAM]
    end
    subgraph SFR[Special Function Registers 128 Bytes]
        S1[80H P0]
        S2[90H P1]
        S3[A0H P2]
        S4[B0H P3]
        S5[88H TCON]
        S6[89H TMOD]
        S7[D0H PSW]
        S8[E0H ACC]
        S9[F0H B]
    end
    ROM --- RAM
    RAM --- SFR
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** The RAM (00H–7FH) and SFR (80H–FFH) share the same 16-bit address space but are separated by the addressing instruction itself. **Direct addressing to 00H–7FH → RAM; 80H–FFH → SFR.** This is a classic viva trap question.

## 4.3 Generic Program Execution Cycle (Fetch–Decode–Execute)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[PC points to next instruction in ROM] --> B[Fetch opcode from ROM]
    B --> C[Decode opcode in instruction decoder]
    C --> D[Read operands from RAM or SFR]
    D --> E[Execute in ALU]
    E --> F[Update PSW flags]
    F --> G[Write result back to destination]
    G --> H[Increment PC by 1, 2 or 3 bytes]
    H --> A
```

Each loop is **one machine cycle (12 oscillator clocks for the standard 8051)**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**
**Differentiate between the `LCALL` and `ACALL` instructions of 8051. Mention one scenario where each is preferred.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | `LCALL` | `ACALL` |
|---|---|---|
| Opcode size | **3 bytes** | **2 bytes** |
| Target address range | Full 64 KB (0x0000–0xFFFF) | Within 2 KB of current PC (11-bit absolute) |
| Use when | Subroutine is far away in ROM (e.g., in >2 KB range) | Subroutine is nearby, code size matters |

**Scenario:** `LCALL` → calling a complex LCD-handling routine located at 0x3000 from address 0x0050. `ACALL` → calling a delay subroutine right after main code.

*(1 mark for opcode sizes, 1 mark for range, 1 mark for scenario = 3 marks)*

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Explain the function of the PSW (Program Status Word) register of 8051. List its addressable bits.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **PSW** is an 8-bit SFR at address **0xD0**, bit-addressable. It holds the **CPU status flags**:

| Bit | Name | Function |
|---|---|---|
| PSW.7 | **CY** | Carry flag — set on arithmetic overflow/borrow |
| PSW.6 | **AC** | Auxiliary carry — for BCD operations |
| PSW.5 | **F0** | User-definable general-purpose flag |
| PSW.4 / PSW.3 | **RS1, RS0** | Register bank select (00=Bank0 … 11=Bank3) |
| PSW.2 | **OV** | Overflow flag for signed arithmetic |
| PSW.1 | **-** | Reserved (user bit) |
| PSW.0 | **P** | Parity flag — auto-set to make ACC bits even |

*(1 mark for definition + address, 1 mark for CY/AC/OV, 1 mark for RS0/RS1/P = 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

> Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern: answer **one full question** of 14 marks, which contains **two sub-parts of 7 marks each**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **(a) [7 Marks]** — `LCALL`/`ACALL` & Multi-byte Arithmetic

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper, Module 2]**

**Q:** Write an 8051 assembly language program to add **three 8-bit numbers** stored in internal RAM locations **20H, 21H, and 22H**. Store the 8-bit sum in location **30H** and the final carry flag status in **bit-addressable location 00H** (i.e., the LSB of register bank 0). Also write the program to add two **16-bit** numbers stored at memory locations **(50H, 51H)** and **(60H, 61H)** with the 16-bit result stored at **(70H, 71H)**.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Three 8-bit numbers addition:**

```asm
        ORG 0000H
        LJMP MAIN
        ORG 0030H
MAIN:   MOV SP, #60H
        CLR A                ; A = 00H, C = 0
        ADD A, 20H           ; A = A + [20H]
        ADD A, 21H           ; A = A + [21H]
        ADD A, 22H           ; A = A + [22H]
        MOV 30H, A           ; Store 8-bit sum
        MOV 00H, C           ; Store Carry into bit 00H
STOP:   SJMP STOP
        END
```

**Explanation (3 marks for code, 1 mark for explanation, 1 mark for correct flag handling):**
- `CLR A` initializes A and clears carry (since `ADD` does not auto-clear it).
- Three sequential `ADD` operations accumulate the sum.
- The `MOV 00H, C` uses bit-addressing to store carry into the bit-addressable RAM region.

**(ii) 16-bit addition:**

```asm
        ORG 0000H
        LJMP MAIN
        ORG 0030H
MAIN:   MOV SP, #60H
        CLR C                ; Clear carry before low-byte addition
        MOV A, 50H           ; A = low byte of first number
        ADD A, 60H           ; Add low byte of second number
        MOV 70H, A           ; Store result low byte
        MOV A, 51H           ; A = high byte of first number
        ADDC A, 61H          ; Add high byte + carry
        MOV 71H, A           ; Store result high byte
STOP:   SJMP STOP
        END
```

**(2 marks for correct use of `CLR C` + `ADDC`):**
- `CLR C` ensures no spurious carry from previous operations.
- `ADDC` correctly propagates carry from low to high byte.

---

#### **(b) [7 Marks]** — Delay Calculation & Port Manipulation

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]**

**Q:** Design an 8051 assembly program to blink **LED connected to P1.0** with a duty cycle of approximately **60% ON and 40% OFF**, using an **11.0592 MHz crystal**. Calculate the delay subroutine counts.

**Model Solution:**

**Design equations:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{MC} &= \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^{6}} \approx 1.085 \,\mu s \\
\text{For 500 ms total period (50% reference): } \\
N_{500ms} &= \frac{500 \times 10^{-3}}{1.085 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 460{,}830 \text{ MC} \\
\text{ON time (60%) } &\Rightarrow T_{ON} \approx 300 \,ms \\
\text{OFF time (40%) } &\Rightarrow T_{OFF} \approx 200 \,ms
\end{aligned}
$$

**Calculations for delay subroutines:**

For **T_ON ≈ 300 ms** (using a nested loop with inner count = 230):

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{ON} &= \frac{300 \times 10^{-3}}{1.085 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 276{,}498 \\
\text{Outer count} &= \frac{276{,}498}{461} \approx 600
\end{aligned}
$$

For **T_OFF ≈ 200 ms**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{OFF} &= \frac{200 \times 10^{-3}}{1.085 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 184{,}332 \\
\text{Outer count} &= \frac{184{,}332}{461} \approx 400
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Program:**

```asm
        ORG 0000H
        LJMP MAIN
        ORG 0030H

DELAY_ON:  MOV R1, #600
LOOP1_ON:  MOV R0, #230
LOOP2_ON:  DJNZ R0, LOOP2_ON
            DJNZ R1, LOOP1_ON
            RET

DELAY_OFF: MOV R1, #400
LOOP1_OFF: MOV R0, #230
LOOP2_OFF: DJNZ R0, LOOP2_OFF
            DJNZ R1, LOOP1_OFF
            RET

MAIN:   MOV SP, #60H
AGAIN:  SETB P1.0           ; LED ON
        LCALL DELAY_ON
        CLR  P1.0           ; LED OFF
        LCALL DELAY_OFF
        SJMP AGAIN
        END
```

**Mark distribution:** 2 marks for delay formula, 2 marks for accurate count calculation, 2 marks for correct program flow, 1 mark for explaining `SETB`/`CLR`.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

#### **(a) [7 Marks]** — Sorting Algorithm on 8051

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper, Module 2]**

**Q:** Write an 8051 assembly program to sort **five unsigned numbers** stored in internal RAM locations starting from **30H** in **ascending order** using the **Bubble Sort** algorithm. Display the smallest value on **Port P2**.

**Model Solution:**

```asm
        ORG 0000H
        LJMP MAIN
        ORG 0030H
MAIN:   MOV SP, #60H
        MOV R7, #04         ; Number of passes = N-1 = 4
OUTER:  MOV R6, #04         ; Inner limit = N-1
        MOV R0, #30H        ; Pointer to start of array
        CLR F0              ; Swap flag in PSW.5
INNER:  MOV A, @R0
        INC R0
        CJNE A, @R0, CHECK   ; If A > next, jump
        SJMP SKIP
CHECK:  JC SKIP              ; If A < next (C=1), no swap
        ; Swap A and @R0
        XCH A, @R0
        DEC R0
        MOV @R0, A
        INC R0
        SETB F0              ; Mark that a swap happened
SKIP:   DJNZ R6, INNER
        JNB F0, DONE         ; If no swap in this pass, sorted
        DJNZ R7, OUTER
DONE:   MOV P2, 30H          ; Display smallest on Port 2
STOP:   SJMP STOP
        END
```

**Explanation (Mark distribution: 4 marks code + 2 marks logic + 1 mark for F0 optimization):**

- The outer loop runs N−1 times; the inner loop compares adjacent pairs.
- `CJNE` followed by `JC` checks if A > next; if yes, swap using `XCH`.
- The `F0` flag-based early termination is a **professional optimization** that stops sorting if no swaps occurred in a pass (array already sorted).

---

#### **(b) [7 Marks]** — BCD to ASCII Conversion

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]**

**Q:** A BCD digit (0–9) is stored at internal RAM location **40H**. Write an 8051 assembly program to convert it to its **ASCII equivalent** and store the result at **50H**. Also explain how the program can be extended to convert a **2-digit BCD number (00–99)** at 40H–41H into ASCII and store at 50H–51H.

**Model Solution (Single-digit):**

```asm
        ORG 0000H
        LJMP MAIN
        ORG 0030H
MAIN:   MOV SP, #60H
        MOV A, 40H          ; Load BCD digit
        ANL A, #0FH         ; Mask upper nibble (safety)
        ORL A, #30H         ; Add ASCII '0' (0x30)
        MOV 50H, A          ; Store ASCII equivalent
STOP:   SJMP STOP
        END
```

**Logic:** ASCII digits '0'–'9' occupy 0x30–0x39. Adding 0x30 (OR-ing is equivalent since upper nibble is 0) converts the BCD value.

**Extension for 2-digit BCD:**

```asm
        MOV A, 40H          ; Tens digit BCD
        ANL A, #0FH
        ORL A, #30H
        MOV 50H, A          ; ASCII tens digit

        MOV A, 41H          ; Ones digit BCD
        ANL A, #0FH
        ORL A, #30H
        MOV 51H, A          ; ASCII ones digit
```

**Mark distribution:** 3 marks single-digit code, 2 marks logic explanation, 2 marks for 2-digit extension.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in 8051 Programming Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting `CLR C` before `ADD`** — costs 1–2 marks silently in multi-byte addition.
> 2. **Using `ADD` instead of `ADDC`** in high-byte addition — application crash, ZERO marks for that sub-question.
> 3. **Not initializing the Stack Pointer (`MOV SP, #60H`)** — leads to unexplained crashes during interrupts/subroutines; examiners deduct 1 mark.
> 4. **Wrong delay count computation** — must show the full formula; merely writing the subroutine without derivation loses 2 of 3 derivation marks.
> 5. **Confusing SFR addresses with RAM addresses** — e.g., writing `MOV A, 90H` instead of `MOV P1, A` works but demonstrates poor practice; some examiners deduct for non-symbolic SFR usage.
> 6. **Missing `END` directive** — program won't assemble in Keil, but examiners often still award partial logic marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- **Program skeleton:** `ORG 0000H` → `LJMP MAIN` → `ORG 0030H` → Code → `END`
- **Always initialize** `SP` to 0x60 or 0x70 before any subroutine call.
- **5 addressing modes:** Immediate, Register, Direct, Register-Indirect, Indexed — `I-R-D-R-I`
- **PSW register:** at 0xD0, bit-addressable, holds CY, AC, F0, RS1, RS0, OV, P.
- **Register banks:** 4 banks of 8 registers each (R0–R7), selected via RS1:RS0 in PSW. Bank 0 default.
- **Stack:** grows **upward** in 8051 (unlike 8085/8086 which grow downward).
- **Delay formula:** $T = MC \times T_{machine}$ where $T_{machine} = 12/f_{osc}$
- **Multi-byte addition rule:** `CLR C` before low byte, `ADD` for low byte, `ADDC` for all subsequent bytes.
- **LCALL** = 3 bytes (full 64 KB), **ACALL** = 2 bytes (2 KB page).
- **SFRs and upper RAM share addresses 80H–FFH** but are differentiated by the addressing mode used.
- **Bit-addressable region:** RAM 20H–2FH (16 bytes = 128 bits) + SFRs ending in 0/8 (e.g., P0, P1, TCON).
- **Common KTU lab programs:** LED blink, key scanning, 7-segment multiplexing, LCD interfacing, ADC/DAC, stepper motor control, UART communication, timer-based delays.
- **Embedded C keywords to know:** `sfr`, `sbit`, `code`, `idata`, `xdata`, `pdata`, `bit`, `using` (for register bank).
- **Crystal for UART:** 11.0592 MHz is **industry standard** because it gives exact 9600 baud rate with zero error.
- **DPTR usage:** 16-bit data pointer for ROM table lookups via `MOVC A, @A+DPTR`.
- **Viva mantra:** If the code doesn't include `END` and `SP` initialization, it's not a complete 8051 program — even if the logic is perfect.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design and Implement Various Interfaces to the 8051 Microcontroller
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCECL408 - MICROCONTROLLERS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Design and Implement Various Interfaces to the 8051 Microcontroller # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Interfacing** in the context of the **8051 Microcontroller** refers to the design methodology and hardware/software protocols required to establish a reliable communication pathway between the microcontroller's General Purpose Input/Output (GPIO) ports and external peripheral devices (actuators, sensors, displays, and motors). The 8051, being an 8-bit Harvard architecture microcontroller with limited drive current (≈ **10–20 mA** per pin, sink/source), cannot directly drive high-power or high-voltage loads. Therefore, interfacing circuits (driver stages, buffers, optocouplers, and pull-up resistors) become mandatory. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PCECL408):** Module 2 explicitly covers the design and implementation of LED, switch (debounced), 7-segment display, LCD (16x2 HD44780), 4x4 matrix keypad, stepper motor (unipolar/bipolar), DC motor (PWM via 8051), and ADC (ADC0804) / DAC (DAC0808) interfaces using both **Assembly Language (ASM51)** and **Embedded C (Keil µVision)**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of the **8051 microcontroller as the "brain"** of an embedded system. Just as a human brain cannot directly connect to a light bulb without nerves, muscles, and spinal circuitry, the 8051 cannot directly drive heavy electrical loads. The **interfacing circuitry acts as the "nervous system"** — translating the brain's low-power digital commands into the high-current/high-voltage signals that real-world devices demand. For example: - The **brain's thought** ("turn on LED") → 8051 sets **Port 1, Pin 0** HIGH. - The **nerve signal** → a current-limiting resistor (≈ 330 Ω) drops the voltage from 5 V to ≈ 2 V forward bias for the LED. - The **muscle** → LED glows because current (≈ 10 mA) flows safely. Without this "nervous system" (resistor, transistor driver, or driver IC like **ULN2003**), the brain's signal either does nothing or destroys itself. ## 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics for 8051 Interfacing | Metric | Standard Value | Notes | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | VOH (Logic High Output) | **≥ 2.4 V** | Minimum guaranteed HIGH output | | VOL (Logic Low Output) | **≤ 0.45 V** | Maximum guaranteed LOW output | | IOH (Source Current per pin) | **≈ -10 mA** | Current sourced when HIGH | | IOL (Sink Current per pin) | **≈ 10–20 mA** | Current sunk when LOW (Port 0 higher) | | VIH (Input HIGH threshold) | **≥ 2.0 V** | Recognized as logic 1 | | VIL (Input LOW threshold) | **≤ 0.8 V** | Recognized as logic 0 | | Crystal Frequency | **11.0592 MHz** | Standard for serial baud rate generation | | Machine Cycle | **12 oscillator periods** | 1 µs @ 11.0592 MHz | > [!NOTE] > **Pull-up Resistor Rule of Thumb:** Port 0 has **open-drain** outputs and ALWAYS requires external pull-ups (typically **10 kΩ** for digital lines) for interfacing. Ports 1, 2, 3 have internal pull-ups (≈ 30–50 kΩ weak) but benefit from external 4.7 kΩ–10 kΩ for noise-immune industrial designs. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The "Why & How" of Common 8051 Interfaces ### 2.1.1 LED Interfacing - **Why:** Visual indicator for logic state, diagnostics, status. - **How:** Anode → current-limiting resistor → Vcc (5 V). Cathode → 8051 pin. Driving pin **LOW** sinks current → LED ON (active-LOW configuration is preferred because 8051 sinks more current than it sources). - **Resistor calculation:** Using Ohm's Law. $$R_{LED} = \frac{V_{CC} - V_{F(LED)}}{I_{LED}}$$ where typical $V_F = 1.8\text{ V}$ (red) and $I_{LED} = 10\text{ mA}$. ### 2.1.2 Switch (Debounced) Interfacing - **Why:** Mechanical switches produce **contact bounce** (≈ 5–20 ms), causing false multiple triggers. - **How:** Two methods: - **Hardware:** RC low-pass filter (R = 10 kΩ, C = 1 µF → τ = 10 ms) followed by **Schmitt trigger** (74LS14). - **Software:** After detecting a press, wait 20–30 ms, then re-read; if still pressed, accept it. ### 2.1.3 Seven-Segment Display Interfacing - **Why:** Display decimal digits (0–9) using 7 LEDs + 1 decimal point. - **How:** Two modes: - **Common Cathode (CC):** Common pin to GND; segments driven HIGH via current-limiting resistors (≈ 330 Ω). - **Common Anode (CA):** Common pin to Vcc; segments driven LOW. - **Multiplexing:** Time-division multiplexing reduces pin count. With **n** displays, the refresh frequency must be $> 50\text{ Hz} \times n$ to avoid flicker. ### 2.1.4 LCD (16x2 HD44780) Interfacing - **Why:** Display alphanumeric data, requires only 6 I/O lines in 4-bit mode. - **How:** Two modes: - **8-bit mode:** Uses 8 data lines (D0–D7) + RS + EN + RW = 11 lines. - **4-bit mode (preferred):** Uses D4–D7 + RS + EN + RW = 7 lines. Data sent in two nibbles (high nibble first). - **Initialization sequence:** Wait 15 ms → 0x30 → 4.1 ms → 0x30 → 100 µs → 0x30 → 0x20 (switch to 4-bit) → Function Set → Display ON → Clear Display → Entry Mode Set. ### 2.1.5 4x4 Matrix Keypad Interfacing - **Why:** 16 keys using only 8 lines (4 rows + 4 columns) instead of 16. - **How:** 1. Configure columns as **outputs** (drive LOW one at a time), rows as **inputs with pull-ups**. 2. Scan each column: drive a column LOW, read rows; the row pulled LOW identifies the pressed key. 3. Debounce in software (20 ms delay). ### 2.1.6 Stepper Motor Interfacing - **Why:** Precise angular positioning (e.g., 1.8°/step in a 200-step motor). - **How:** Energize stator coils in a specific sequence (full-step, half-step). Driver IC **ULN2003A** (Darlington array, 500 mA per channel) handles current amplification. The 8051's Port pins → ULN2003 → motor coils. ### 2.1.7 DC Motor Interfacing (PWM) - **Why:** Variable speed control. - **How:** Pulse Width Modulation on the enable pin of an **H-bridge driver** (L293D or L298N). Frequency ≈ **1 kHz** (above audible range). Duty cycle 0–100% controls speed. ### 2.1.8 ADC0804 Interfacing - **Why:** Convert analog sensor signals (temperature, light) to 8-bit digital. - **How:** 8051 reads ADC0804 via parallel bus. Connect INTR pin to an external interrupt (INT0/INT1) for end-of-conversion notification. Use **RD** signal to read the digital byte. ### 2.1.9 DAC0808 Interfacing - **Why:** Generate analog waveforms (sine, triangular) from digital codes. - **How:** 8051 latches a digital byte on Port 1 → DAC0808 → I-to-V converter (op-amp LM358) → analog output. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Concept | Formula / Value | Unit | Remarks | | :-: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | LED current-limit resistor | $R = \frac{V_{CC} - V_F}{I_F}$ | Ω | Use $V_{CC} = 5\text{ V}$, $I_F \le 10\text{ mA}$ | | 2 | 8051 Machine Cycle | $T_{MC} = \frac{12}{f_{OSC}}$ | seconds | 1 µs @ 11.0592 MHz | | 3 | 8051 Timer Frequency | $f_{timer} = \frac{f_{OSC}}{12 \times (65536 - TH-TL)}$ | Hz | For 1 ms tick @ 11.0592 MHz → TH0 = 0xFC, TL0 = 0x66 | | 4 | Stepper step angle | $\theta_{step} = \frac{360°}{N_{steps}}$ | degrees | E.g., 200 steps → 1.8°/step | | 5 | Stepper torque vs speed | $T \propto \frac{1}{\omega}$ | N·m | Higher speed → lower torque | | 6 | PWM Duty Cycle | $D = \frac{T_{ON}}{T_{ON} + T_{OFF}} \times 100$ | % | Speed of DC motor ∝ D | | 7 | ADC0804 Resolution | $\Delta V = \frac{V_{REF}}{256}$ | V/step | For $V_{REF} = 5\text{ V}$ → 19.53 mV/step | | 8 | ADC0804 Conversion Time | $t_{CONV} = 110 \times C_{CLK}$ | seconds | With $C_{CLK} = 150\text{ pF}$, ≈ 110 µs | | 9 | DAC0808 Output Current | $I_{OUT} = \frac{V_{REF}}{R_{REF}} \times \frac{D}{256}$ | A | D = digital input byte | | 10 | Debounce delay | $t_{db} \ge 20\text{ ms}$ | ms | Standard mechanical bounce window | | 11 | Multiplexed display refresh | $f_{refresh} \ge 50 \times n$ | Hz | $n$ = number of digits | | 12 | Keypad scan time | $t_{scan} \le 20\text{ ms}$ total | ms | Avoid key-press loss | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** These interfaces constitute the **physical layer** of every real embedded product — from washing machines (LCD + keypad + motor) to industrial PLCs (ADC + DAC + relays). Mastering them is equivalent to learning the alphabet before writing prose. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivative 1: LED Current-Limit Resistor Calculation **Problem (Typical KTU viva question):** An LED with forward voltage $V_F = 2.0\text{ V}$ and forward current $I_F = 15\text{ mA}$ is interfaced to P1.0 of the 8051 in active-LOW configuration. Calculate the current-limiting resistor. Assume $V_{CC} = 5.0\text{ V}$ and $V_{OL} = 0.45\text{ V}$. **Step 1 — Identify the circuit topology.** In active-LOW, the LED anode connects to $V_{CC}$ through resistor R, and cathode connects to P1.0. When P1.0 outputs LOW, current flows from $V_{CC}$ → R → LED → P1.0 → GND. **Step 2 — Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) around the loop.** $$V_{CC} = V_R + V_F + V_{OL}$$ This states that the supply voltage equals the sum of voltage drops across the resistor, LED, and the microcontroller's output pin in the LOW state. **Step 3 — Substitute the known values.** $$5.0 = V_R + 2.0 + 0.45$$ **Step 4 — Solve for $V_R$ (voltage across the resistor).** $$V_R = 5.0 - 2.0 - 0.45 = 2.55\text{ V}$$ This is the voltage that the resistor must drop to limit the current to the desired value. **Step 5 — Apply Ohm's Law to find R.** $$R = \frac{V_R}{I_F} = \frac{2.55\text{ V}}{15\text{ mA}} = \frac{2.55}{0.015}$$ **Step 6 — Numerical evaluation.** $$R = 170\text{ Ω}$$ **Step 7 — Choose the next standard E12 value.** $$R_{standard} = 180\text{ Ω} \quad (\text{safe, slightly lower } I_F \approx 14.2\text{ mA})$$ > [!NOTE] > If the active-HIGH configuration is used instead, the equation becomes $V_{CC} = V_{OH} + V_R + V_F$, where $V_{OH} = 2.4\text{ V}$. Re-deriving: $V_R = 5.0 - 2.4 - 2.0 = 0.6\text{ V}$, so $R = 0.6/0.015 = 40\text{ Ω}$. Since 40 Ω is below safe values, **active-LOW is preferred** for direct 8051 LED drive. ## 3.2 Derivative 2: ADC0804 Digital Output for a Given Analog Input **Problem:** The ADC0804 is configured with $V_{REF}/2 = 2.5\text{ V}$, so its full-scale analog input range is 0–5 V.
